**SysTick.** Most MCUs include a small standard timer called **SysTick** dedicated to this job. It is the usual source of the regular "system tick" (commonly every 1 ms).

**Building a clock from ticks.** Keep a variable and add 1 to it in the tick handler. With a 1 ms tick, that variable counts milliseconds since power-on. That is exactly what the popular `millis()` function returns; counting a faster tick gives `micros()` (microseconds, millionths of a second).

**Recap:** the auto-reload value sets how high the counter goes before wrapping and firing a periodic event; counting those events lets you keep time.

items.prompt: What does the auto-reload value (ARR) decide?
choices: How high the counter climbs before it wraps back to 0 and fires an event
How fast the chip's main clock runs
Which pin the timer output appears on
The voltage level of the output
items.explanation: The auto-reload value is the top of the count. Reaching it makes the counter wrap to 0 and fire the period event. It does not change the clock speed or pick pins.

items.prompt: What happens when an up-counting timer reaches its auto-reload value and gets one more tick?
choices: It wraps back to 0 and fires an event (which can trigger an interrupt)
It stops permanently and must be reset by hand
It keeps climbing past the top with no limit
It doubles its clock speed
items.explanation: At the top, the next tick wraps the counter to 0 and generates the period event, optionally causing an interrupt. It does not halt forever or count past the top.

items.prompt: The counter advances at 1,000,000 ticks per second and the auto-reload value is 999. How often does the period event fire?
choices: 1000 times per second (once every 1 ms)
Once per second
1,000,000 times per second
999 times per second
items.explanation: It wraps every (999 + 1) = 1000 ticks. 1,000,000 / 1000 = 1000 events per second, i.e. once every millisecond. The +1 matters: there are 1000 steps including 0.

items.prompt: A chip clock is 8,000,000 ticks/second. The prescaler divides by 8, and the auto-reload value is 999. What is the period event rate?
choices: 1000 events per second (every 1 ms)
8000 events per second
100 events per second
1,000,000 events per second
items.explanation: Counter rate = 8,000,000 / 8 = 1,000,000 per second. Wrap rate = 1,000,000 / (999 + 1) = 1000 per second. So one event every 1 ms.

items.prompt: Why is the number of steps per period (auto-reload + 1) rather than just the auto-reload value?
choices: Because counting starts at 0, so values 0 through ARR are (ARR + 1) distinct steps
Because the clock always adds one extra tick at random
Because the prescaler adds one
It is a mistake; the period is exactly ARR steps
items.explanation: The counter visits 0, 1, 2, ..., ARR before wrapping. Counting 0 itself, that is ARR + 1 values. Forgetting the +1 is a classic off-by-one timing error.

items.prompt: What is SysTick typically used for?
choices: Providing a regular system tick (often every 1 ms) to keep time and pace the program
Generating analog audio
Storing the program permanently
Supplying power to the chip
items.explanation: SysTick is a small standard timer found in most MCUs, used to produce the regular system tick that drives timekeeping and scheduling. It is not for audio, storage, or power.

items.prompt: How do you build a millis() clock (milliseconds since power-on) from a 1 ms periodic tick?
choices: Add 1 to a counter variable inside the tick handler each time the tick fires
Read the chip's temperature each tick
Multiply the prescaler by the clock every loop
Reset the timer to 0 every second
items.explanation: Each 1 ms tick runs a tiny handler that increments a counter; that counter is the elapsed milliseconds. The other options do not accumulate elapsed time.

items.prompt: What is the difference between millis() and micros()?
choices: millis() counts milliseconds; micros() counts microseconds (a thousand times finer)
millis() is for motors; micros() is for LEDs
They are the same function with different names
micros() counts hours, millis() counts minutes
items.explanation: A millisecond is 1/1000 of a second; a microsecond is 1/1,000,000. micros() gives finer resolution by counting a faster tick. Neither is tied to a specific device.

items.prompt: An interrupt fired by the timer's period event does what?
choices: Briefly pauses the CPU's normal work to run a short handler, then returns
Permanently stops the program
Slows the chip clock in half
Erases the counter's value from memory forever
items.explanation: An interrupt is a signal that temporarily diverts the CPU to a small handler routine and then resumes normal execution. It does not halt the program or alter the clock.

lessons.title: PWM: Controlling Average Power by Switching On and Off
lessons.content:
Here is a neat trick. Imagine dimming a light by flipping its switch on and off so fast your eye cannot see the flicker; it just looks dimmer. A microwave on "50% power" does the same thing: it runs full power, then off, then on, cycling so the food gets about half the energy. This is **Pulse-Width Modulation**, or **PWM**.

**How it works.** A timer switches a pin ON and OFF very quickly. Each on/off cycle has the same length (the period). What you change is the **duty cycle**: the fraction of each cycle that the pin is ON.

$$\text{duty cycle} = \frac{\text{time ON}}{\text{whole period}}$$

A 25% duty cycle means ON one quarter of the time; 75% means ON three quarters of the time.

**Why it matters.** Because the switching is so fast, what a device (and your eye) feels is the **average** power. More ON time = higher average. So:

- An **LED** at 25% duty looks dim; at 75% it looks bright. (Dimming.)
- A **motor** at higher duty spins faster, because it gets more average power.
- A **servo** (a motor that turns to a commanded angle) reads the *width* of the ON pulse to decide its position, e.g. a 1 ms pulse vs a 2 ms pulse points it to different angles.

**Setting the duty.** Inside the timer, a compare value decides the switch point: the pin is ON while the counter is below it and OFF above it. Raise that value and the pin stays ON longer. The period (frequency) stays the same; only the ON fraction moves.

**Alignment, briefly.** "Edge-aligned" PWM lines pulses up at the start of each period and is fine for LEDs and servos. "Center-aligned" centers the pulse and is smoother for driving motors. You can ignore the details for now.

**Recap:** PWM switches a pin on and off fast; the duty cycle (ON fraction) sets the average power, letting you dim LEDs, set motor speed, or position a servo.

items.prompt: What does PWM (Pulse-Width Modulation) actually do to a pin?
choices: Switches it ON and OFF rapidly, varying the fraction of time it is ON
Slowly raises the exact voltage to any level you like
Connects the pin to the chip clock directly
Reads how much current flows into the pin
items.explanation: PWM rapidly toggles the pin between fully ON and fully OFF, changing the ON fraction (duty cycle). It does not produce a smooth adjustable voltage directly, and it is an output technique, not a measurement.

items.prompt: What is the duty cycle?
choices: The fraction of each cycle that the pin is ON
The number of cycles per second
The total voltage of the supply
How many timers the chip has
items.explanation: Duty cycle = time ON divided by the whole period. The number of cycles per second is the frequency, a different quantity.

items.prompt: You raise an LED's PWM duty cycle from 25% to 75%. What happens to its brightness, and why?
choices: It gets brighter, because a higher duty cycle delivers more average power
It gets dimmer, because more switching wastes power
Nothing changes, because the frequency stayed the same
It turns off, because 75% is too high
items.explanation: Higher duty means the LED is ON a larger fraction of the time, so its average power (and apparent brightness) goes up. Frequency staying constant is exactly why only the average changed.

items.prompt: Why does PWM let you control average power instead of just full-on or full-off?
choices: The switching is so fast that the device responds to the average of ON and OFF time
PWM changes the supply voltage to a fraction
PWM heats the pin to reduce its output
PWM only ever delivers exactly half power
items.explanation: Because it toggles faster than the device (or your eye) can react, what is felt is the average level, set by the duty cycle. The supply voltage itself is not changed.

items.prompt: A microwave set to 50% power cycles fully on, then off, then on, and so on. This is an everyday example of:
choices: PWM: controlling average power by varying ON time, not by halving the actual power
A broken microwave
Lowering the wall voltage to half
Input capture measuring the food
items.explanation: The microwave delivers full power part of the time and none the rest, averaging to about half. That is exactly the PWM idea: average power set by duty, not a true half-power setting.

items.prompt: A PWM signal has a period of 20 ms and a duty cycle of 25%. How long is the pin ON during each cycle?
choices: 5 ms
25 ms
20 ms
items.explanation: ON time = duty x period = 0.25 x 20 ms = 5 ms. The remaining 15 ms it is OFF. Duty is a fraction of the period, not a number of milliseconds by itself.

items.prompt: How does a hobby servo decide what angle to turn to?
choices: From the width of the ON pulse it receives (e.g. 1 ms vs 2 ms picks different angles)
From the color of the wire
From the chip's temperature
From how many pins are connected
items.explanation: A servo reads the ON-pulse width and moves to the matching position; common servos use roughly 1 to 2 ms pulses for their range. The other options have nothing to do with servo control.

items.prompt: Inside the timer, raising the compare value (the switch point) while keeping the auto-reload the same will:
choices: Increase the duty cycle (pin ON longer) without changing the frequency
Increase the frequency without changing the duty
Turn the timer off
Halve the clock speed
items.explanation: The pin is ON while the counter is below the compare value. A higher compare value keeps it ON longer each period, raising duty. The period (set by auto-reload) and thus the frequency stay put.

items.prompt: Driving a small DC motor with PWM, what does increasing the duty cycle do?
choices: Increases the average power, so the motor spins faster
Reverses the motor's direction
Lowers the average power, slowing it
Changes the PWM frequency
items.explanation: More ON time means more average power to the motor, so it speeds up. Direction is set by polarity, not duty, and a higher duty does not reduce power or alter the frequency.

items.prompt: Edge-aligned versus center-aligned PWM mainly differ in:
choices: Where the ON pulse sits in each period; center-aligned is smoother for motor drive
Whether the pin turns on at all
The number of pins used
The color of the output signal
items.explanation: Both produce the same duty; edge-aligned puts the pulse at the period's start, center-aligned centers it (smoother for motors). It is a placement detail, not whether the pin works.

lessons.title: Input Capture: Measuring an Incoming Pulse
lessons.content:
So far the timer has been making things happen. It can also *measure* things coming in. This is called **input capture**.

**The idea.** Suppose a sensor sends a pulse and you want to know how long it lasts, or how often pulses arrive. You could try to watch the pin in software with a stopwatch, but that is jittery and ties up the CPU. Input capture lets the hardware do it precisely.

**How it works.** You point a timer channel at an input pin and tell it to watch for an **edge** (a moment the signal switches, either a rising edge OFF-to-ON or a falling edge ON-to-OFF). The moment that edge happens, the timer **copies its current count** into a capture register. That copied number is a timestamp: "the count was this when the edge arrived."

**Measuring pulse width.** Capture the count at the rising edge, then again at the falling edge. Subtract the two. The difference is the pulse width, measured in ticks. Multiply by the tick duration to get real time.

$$\text{pulse width} = (\text{count at end} - \text{count at start}) \times \text{seconds per tick}$$

Example: the counter ticks every 1 microsecond. Rising edge captured 1200, falling edge captured 1700. Width = (1700 - 1200) = 500 ticks = 500 microseconds.

**Measuring frequency.** Capture two rising edges in a row. The difference is the period (one full cycle) in ticks. Frequency is just 1 divided by the period. A pulse arriving every 500 microseconds is 1 / 0.0005 s = 2000 times per second (2 kHz).

**One catch: wrap-around.** The counter can wrap back to 0 (from the last lesson) between two edges. If the gap is long, you must also count how many times it wrapped and add those, or your measured time will be too short.

**Real uses:** timing an ultrasonic distance sensor's echo, reading a wheel-speed (tachometer) sensor, or decoding a signal's frequency.

**Recap:** input capture stamps the timer's count at each chosen edge; subtracting timestamps gives a pulse's width or period precisely, in hardware.

items.prompt: What does input capture do?
choices: Copies (timestamps) the timer's count the instant a chosen edge appears on an input pin
Sets the duty cycle of an output pin
Stores program code into flash
Generates a steady output frequency
items.explanation: Input capture latches the current count when an edge arrives, giving a precise timestamp. Setting duty and generating frequency are output (compare/PWM) jobs, not capture.

items.prompt: In this context, what is an "edge"?
choices: The moment a signal switches level (OFF-to-ON is a rising edge, ON-to-OFF is a falling edge)
The physical corner of the chip package
The highest voltage the pin can reach
The boundary of the program's memory
items.explanation: An edge is a transition of the signal level. Input capture triggers on rising or falling edges. It is not a physical chip corner or a voltage limit.

items.prompt: The counter ticks every 1 microsecond. A rising edge is captured at count 1200 and the following falling edge at 1700. What is the pulse width?
choices: 500 microseconds
1700 microseconds
2900 microseconds
1200 microseconds
items.explanation: Width = (1700 - 1200) ticks = 500 ticks, and each tick is 1 microsecond, so 500 microseconds. You subtract the timestamps; you do not add them or use just one.

items.prompt: Two successive rising edges are captured 500 microseconds apart. What is the signal's frequency?
choices: 2000 Hz (2 kHz)
500 Hz
200 Hz
items.explanation: The gap between successive rising edges is one period = 500 microseconds = 0.0005 s. Frequency = 1 / 0.0005 = 2000 Hz. Frequency is the reciprocal of the period.

items.prompt: Why use input capture instead of watching the pin in a software loop with a stopwatch?
choices: Hardware timestamps the edge precisely and does not tie up the CPU, avoiding jitter
Software loops cannot read pins at all
Input capture is the only way to turn an LED on
Software is always more accurate than hardware
items.explanation: Hardware capture records the exact moment of the edge with no software delay, so timing is precise and the CPU stays free. A software loop adds jitter and busy-waits.

items.prompt: To measure a signal's period (for frequency) rather than its high-pulse width, which two captures do you compare?
choices: Two edges of the same kind in a row (e.g. rising to the next rising)
A rising edge and the very next falling edge
Two captures from two different pins
The same single capture twice
items.explanation: Rising-to-next-rising spans one full cycle (the period). Rising-to-falling measures only the high time (pulse width), not the whole period.

items.prompt: If the time between two edges is long, the counter may wrap back to 0 in between. What must your code do?
choices: Count the wraps and add their full counts, or the measured time will be too short
Ignore it; the count never wraps
Double the clock speed automatically
Switch the pin to an output
items.explanation: Each wrap means a full counter range elapsed that the simple subtraction misses. You add those wrapped counts back in, otherwise the measurement is too small. The counter certainly can wrap.

items.prompt: The number copied into the capture register at an edge represents:
choices: A timestamp: what the timer's count was at the instant of that edge
The voltage of the signal in volts
The duty cycle of an output
The chip's serial number
items.explanation: The captured value is the counter's reading at the moment of the edge, i.e. a time stamp in ticks. It is not a voltage or a duty cycle.

items.prompt: Which task is a natural fit for input capture?
choices: Timing the echo pulse from an ultrasonic distance sensor
Setting an LED's brightness
Spinning a motor faster
Storing a calibration value in flash
items.explanation: An ultrasonic sensor reports distance as a pulse whose width you measure, exactly what input capture does. Dimming LEDs and driving motors are PWM (output) tasks; flash storage is unrelated.

lessons.title: Watchdogs, One-Shots, and Timer-Triggered Sampling
lessons.content:
A few more timer jobs round out the picture. These are higher-level uses; you only need the ideas.

**One-shot mode.** Normally a timer reloads and repeats forever (continuous mode). In **one-shot mode** it runs through a single period and then stops. That is handy for a one-time precise delay or a single trigger pulse.

**Watchdog timers.** Firmware sometimes hangs (gets stuck in a loop and stops responding). A **watchdog timer** guards against this. It is a countdown timer that will **reset the whole chip** if it ever reaches zero. Healthy firmware must regularly restart it before it expires, a habit called *kicking* (or *feeding*) the watchdog. If the program hangs, the kicks stop, the watchdog hits zero, and it reboots the system back to a known good state. Think of a smoke alarm you must press "OK" on every minute, otherwise it assumes trouble and acts.

- An **independent watchdog** runs from its own separate clock, so it still works even if the main clock fails.
- A **window watchdog** is stricter: it complains if you kick too *early* as well as too late, catching code that is running wrong, not just code that is stuck.
- Best practice: kick the watchdog from your real main work, not from inside a timer interrupt. A timer interrupt can keep firing and kicking even while the main program is hung, which would hide the very fault the watchdog exists to catch.

**Timer-triggered ADC and DMA.** An **ADC** (Analog-to-Digital Converter) turns a real-world voltage (like a temperature reading) into a number. To sample it at perfectly even intervals, you let a timer's period event *trigger* each conversion automatically, instead of the CPU asking each time. Then **DMA** (Direct Memory Access, a helper that moves data without the CPU) copies each result straight into memory. The result: a stream of evenly spaced samples collected with zero CPU effort and no timing jitter. This even, hands-off sampling is the foundation of clean signal measurement.

**Recap:** one-shot mode fires once and stops; a watchdog reboots a hung system unless regularly kicked; and a timer can trigger an ADC (with DMA) to collect perfectly even samples on its own.

items.prompt: What does one-shot mode do differently from continuous mode?
choices: It runs through a single period and then stops, instead of repeating forever
It counts twice as fast
It never fires an event
It controls two pins at once
items.explanation: One-shot runs one period and halts, useful for a single delay or trigger pulse. Continuous mode reloads and repeats. Speed and pin count are unrelated to this setting.

items.prompt: What problem does a watchdog timer solve?
choices: It reboots the chip if the firmware hangs and stops responding
It dims LEDs smoothly
It measures incoming pulse widths
It stores the program in flash
items.explanation: A watchdog resets the system when firmware gets stuck, returning it to a known good state. Dimming is PWM, measuring pulses is input capture, and storage is unrelated.

items.prompt: What does it mean to "kick" (or feed) the watchdog?
choices: Restart its countdown before it reaches zero, to show the firmware is still healthy
Turn the watchdog off permanently
Reset the entire chip on purpose
Increase the PWM duty cycle
items.explanation: Kicking restarts the watchdog's countdown, signalling that the program is alive. If kicks stop, the countdown expires and the chip reboots. Kicking does not disable it or reboot the chip itself.

items.prompt: The firmware hangs in an endless loop and never kicks the watchdog again. What happens?
choices: The watchdog reaches zero and resets the chip back to a known good state
Nothing; the watchdog waits forever
The watchdog speeds up the CPU to escape the loop
The watchdog deletes the buggy code
items.explanation: With no kicks, the watchdog times out and reboots the system, recovering from the hang. It cannot fix the bug or speed up the CPU; it just forces a reset.

items.prompt: Why does an independent watchdog run from its own separate clock?
choices: So it still works to reset the chip even if the main clock fails
So it can dim LEDs without the timer
So it runs slower than everything else for fun
So it can store more data
items.explanation: A separate clock means the watchdog keeps running even if the main clock stops, covering a serious failure mode. The other options are unrelated to its purpose.

items.prompt: How is a window watchdog stricter than an ordinary one?
choices: It also faults if you kick it too early, not just too late, catching code running wrong
It never resets the chip
It only works while the LED is on
It requires no kicking at all
items.explanation: A window watchdog defines an allowed time window; kicking too soon is treated as a fault, which catches misbehaving code that runs too fast or skips steps. An ordinary watchdog only minds being kicked too late.

items.prompt: Why is it bad practice to kick the watchdog from inside a timer interrupt?
choices: The interrupt may keep firing and kicking even while the main program is hung, hiding the fault
Interrupts cannot access the watchdog at all
It would make the LED too bright
It would speed up the chip clock
items.explanation: A timer interrupt can keep running independently of the main loop, so it would keep kicking even during a hang, defeating the watchdog. Kick from real main-line work instead.

items.prompt: What does an ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter) do?
choices: Turns a real-world voltage into a number the chip can use
Turns a number into a PWM signal
Stores the program in flash
Generates the chip's clock
items.explanation: An ADC measures an analog voltage (such as from a sensor) and converts it to a digital value. It is the input counterpart to converting numbers back to signals.

items.prompt: Why let a timer trigger each ADC conversion, with DMA storing the results, instead of the CPU requesting each sample?
choices: It gives perfectly even sample spacing with no CPU effort and no timing jitter
It makes the samples random for security
It uses the LED to time the samples
It is the only way an ADC can ever work
items.explanation: A timer fires conversions at exact intervals and DMA moves results without the CPU, so samples are evenly spaced and jitter-free. CPU-requested sampling adds variable delay; an ADC can also be triggered by software, just less precisely.

items.prompt: What is DMA (Direct Memory Access)?
choices: A helper that moves data (e.g. ADC results) into memory without using the CPU
A faster type of CPU
A way to dim an LED
A watchdog clock
items.explanation: DMA transfers data between a peripheral and memory on its own, freeing the CPU. It is not a CPU, an LED dimmer, or a watchdog.

lessons.title: Reading a Sensor: What an ADC (and DAC) Do
lessons.content:
## Hook

Turn a volume knob, and somewhere a chip turns your twist into a number. A thermostat reads a temperature sensor the same way. The trick is that most sensors speak in **voltage** (electrical "pressure", measured in volts, V), but a computer program can only work with **numbers**. Something has to translate.

## Build

That translator is the **ADC**, short for **analog-to-digital converter**. "Analog" means a smoothly varying signal, like a voltage that can be 1.0 V, 1.65 V, or anything in between. "Digital" means a plain whole number. The ADC measures the voltage on a pin and hands your program a number that stands for it.

A microcontroller (MCU) is the small computer chip running your program. Most MCUs have an ADC built in, so you can wire a sensor straight to a pin and read it.

What kind of sensors? A temperature sensor whose voltage rises as it gets warmer. A light sensor that gives more voltage in bright light. A **potentiometer** (a knob, often shortened to "pot") whose voltage changes as you turn it. In every case, the ADC reads the voltage and gives you a count.

The **DAC** (**digital-to-analog converter**) does the exact reverse: your program writes a number, and the DAC sets a real voltage on an output pin. That is how a chip can make a tone or a smooth control signal.

## Recap

Sensors give voltages; programs want numbers. The ADC converts voltage to a number, and the DAC converts a number back to a voltage.

items.prompt: What does an ADC do?
choices: Converts a voltage on a pin into a number your program can read
Converts a number from your program into a voltage on a pin
Amplifies a weak signal so it is louder
Stores numbers in flash memory
items.explanation: ADC stands for analog-to-digital converter: voltage in, number out. The reverse (number to voltage) is the DAC. Amplifying and storing are different jobs entirely.

items.prompt: A DAC (digital-to-analog converter) is used to:
choices: Read the voltage from a temperature sensor
Set a real voltage on an output pin from a number your program chooses
Count how many times a button is pressed
Convert a number into text for a display
items.explanation: A DAC takes a number and outputs a matching voltage, which is how an MCU can generate a tone or a smooth control signal. Reading a sensor is the ADC's job.

items.prompt: Why can't your program use a sensor's output directly without an ADC?
choices: Sensors are too slow for software
The sensor gives a voltage, but the program works with numbers, so a translation is needed
Sensors only work with a DAC
The voltage is always exactly zero
items.explanation: Most sensors produce an analog voltage. Software handles whole numbers, so the ADC bridges the two. Speed is not the core issue here.

items.prompt: Which of these is a good everyday example of using an ADC?
choices: Toggling an LED on and off
Reading the position of a volume knob (potentiometer)
Sending a text string over a serial port
Blinking at a fixed rate using a timer
items.explanation: A potentiometer gives a voltage that depends on how far it is turned, and the ADC turns that into a number. Toggling an LED, sending text, and blinking do not involve measuring an analog voltage.

items.prompt: In this context, what does "analog" mean?
choices: A signal that is either fully on or fully off
A smoothly varying signal, like a voltage that can take any value in a range
A number stored in memory
A type of computer chip
items.explanation: Analog signals vary smoothly and can take any value in their range (such as 1.65 V). On/off is a digital idea, not analog.

items.prompt: Where does the ADC usually live in a typical microcontroller project?
choices: On a separate chip you must always buy
Built into the MCU, so a sensor can wire straight to a pin
Only inside the sensor itself
In the power supply
items.explanation: Most MCUs include an ADC on-chip, letting you connect a sensor directly to an ADC-capable pin. External ADCs exist but are not the common starting point.

items.prompt: A thermostat reads a temperature sensor whose voltage rises as the room warms. What converts that voltage into something the thermostat's program can compare to a setpoint?
choices: The DAC
The ADC
A timer
The output pin driver
items.explanation: The rising voltage is analog; the ADC turns it into a number the program can compare against the target. The DAC goes the other direction.

items.prompt: You want your MCU to play a simple tone out of a speaker by producing a changing voltage. Which peripheral fits best?
choices: The ADC, reading the speaker
The DAC, outputting a changing voltage
A button input
The flash memory
items.explanation: Generating an output voltage from numbers is the DAC's job. The ADC only reads voltages; it cannot drive a speaker.

lessons.title: Reference Voltage, Resolution, and Counts to Volts
lessons.content:
## Hook

The ADC gives you a number, but a number alone is meaningless. Is a reading of 2048 a lot or a little? To make sense of it you need two settings: the **reference voltage** and the **resolution**.

## Build

The **reference voltage** (often written $V_{ref}$) is the highest voltage the ADC can measure. Think of it as the top of a ruler. If $V_{ref}$ is 3.3 V, then 0 V reads as the smallest count and 3.3 V reads as the largest count. Anything above $V_{ref}$ just pins at the top.

**Resolution** is how many steps the ruler is divided into, set by a number of **bits**. An $N$-bit ADC has $2^N$ steps. A common choice is **12-bit**, giving $2^{12} = 4096$ steps numbered **0 to 4095**. More bits means finer steps.

Each step is called one **LSB** (least significant bit), the smallest voltage change the ADC can tell apart:

$$\text{step size} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^N}$$

For 12-bit with $V_{ref} = 3.3\,\text{V}$: $3.3 / 4096 \approx 0.81\,\text{mV}$ per count.

To turn a **count back into volts**, multiply by the step size:

$$V_{in} = \text{count} \times \frac{V_{ref}}{2^N} = \frac{\text{count}}{2^N} \times V_{ref}$$

So a reading of 2048 (exactly halfway up the 0..4095 range) means about $2048/4096 \times 3.3 = 1.65\,\text{V}$, half of $V_{ref}$.

## Recap

$V_{ref}$ sets the top of the scale, resolution sets how many steps fit under it, and `count/2^N x Vref` turns any reading back into volts.

items.prompt: A 12-bit ADC has a 3.3 V reference. A reading of 2048 corresponds to about what voltage?
items.explanation: 12-bit means 0..4095, and 2048 is the halfway count. V = (2048/4096) x 3.3 = 1.65 V, exactly half of Vref. 0.81 mV is the step size, not this reading.

items.prompt: For a 12-bit ADC with a 3.3 V reference, what is the step size (one LSB)?
choices: About 0.81 mV per count
About 3.3 mV per count
About 1.65 V per count
About 4096 mV per count
items.explanation: Step size = Vref / 2^N = 3.3 V / 4096, which is about 0.81 mV per count. 1.65 V is the midscale reading, not the step.

items.prompt: How many distinct steps does a 12-bit ADC have?
choices: 12 steps
1024 steps
4096 steps
65536 steps
items.explanation: An N-bit ADC has 2^N steps. 2^12 = 4096, numbered 0 to 4095. 1024 is 2^10 and 65536 is 2^16.

items.prompt: What does the reference voltage (Vref) of an ADC represent?
choices: The lowest voltage the ADC can measure
The highest voltage the ADC can measure, mapped to the largest count
The voltage the ADC outputs to a sensor
The speed of the conversion
items.explanation: Vref is the top of the measuring scale: it maps to the maximum count, while 0 V maps to count 0. The ADC does not output voltage to the sensor, and Vref has nothing to do with speed.

items.prompt: A 10-bit ADC uses a 5.0 V reference. About how many volts is each step (one LSB)?
choices: About 4.9 mV
About 0.5 mV
About 50 mV
About 5 V
items.explanation: 10-bit gives 2^10 = 1024 steps. 5.0 V / 1024 is about 4.9 mV per count.

items.prompt: Keeping Vref the same, you switch an ADC from 8-bit to 12-bit resolution. What happens?
choices: The steps get coarser (bigger volts per count)
The steps get finer (smaller volts per count), so you can tell apart smaller voltage changes
The reference voltage doubles
Nothing changes
items.explanation: More bits means more steps under the same Vref, so each step covers fewer volts and fine changes become visible. Vref is unaffected by the resolution setting.

items.prompt: Your 12-bit, 3.3 V ADC reads a count of 1024. What voltage is on the pin?
choices: About 0.41 V
About 0.825 V
About 1.65 V
About 3.3 V
items.explanation: V = (count/4096) x Vref = (1024/4096) x 3.3 = 0.25 x 3.3 = 0.825 V. 1024 is one quarter of the full 0..4095 range.

items.prompt: Why does the accuracy of the reference voltage matter so much?
choices: It does not matter; only the count matters
Every count is interpreted as a fraction of Vref, so an error in Vref shifts every voltage you calculate
It only matters for the DAC, not the ADC
It sets how fast the ADC can sample
items.explanation: Because V = (count/2^N) x Vref, the converted voltage scales directly with Vref. If Vref is off, all your computed voltages are off by the same factor.

lessons.title: Sampling Time, Conversion Modes, and Triggering
lessons.content:
## Hook

Getting a good reading is not only about resolution. The ADC needs a moment to "look" at the voltage, and you also choose *when* and *how often* it reads. Get these wrong and your numbers drift or arrive at uneven times.

## Build

Inside the ADC is a tiny **sample-and-hold**: a little capacitor (a charge bucket) that must charge up to match the input voltage before the reading is taken. The **sampling time** is how long you give that bucket to fill.

How long is enough depends on the sensor's **source impedance**, roughly how "stiff" or "weak" its output is. A weak source (high impedance, like a simple resistor divider) fills the bucket slowly, so you must set a **longer sampling time** or add a buffer. Too short, and the reading is pulled toward whatever was measured just before.

Next, the **conversion mode**:
- **Single (one-shot):** convert once when asked.
- **Continuous:** the moment one conversion ends, start the next, over and over.
- **Scan / sequence:** step through a list of channels, so you can read several sensors (multi-channel) in one sweep.

Finally, **triggering**, which means what starts a conversion:
- **Software trigger:** your code sets a "start" bit. Easy, but the timing wobbles depending on what the CPU was busy doing.
- **Timer trigger:** a hardware timer fires each conversion at an exact, even rate, with no CPU involvement in the timing.

For evenly spaced samples (for example, to analyze a waveform later), the timer trigger is the right tool: it removes the wobble that a software loop introduces.

## Recap

Give the sample-and-hold enough time (more for weak sources), pick single / continuous / scan to match the job, and let a timer trigger conversions when you need even spacing.

items.prompt: Why is timer-triggering an ADC usually better than reading it in a software loop when you want evenly spaced samples?
choices: The timer increases the ADC resolution
A timer fires each conversion at an exact, fixed rate, while a software loop's timing wobbles with whatever the CPU is doing
A software loop cannot read the ADC at all
The timer lowers the reference voltage
items.explanation: A hardware timer launches conversions at a precise, uniform interval independent of CPU activity. A polling loop's spacing jitters because other code steals time. Triggering does not change resolution or Vref.

items.prompt: What is the job of the sample-and-hold inside the ADC?
choices: To amplify the input voltage
To charge a small capacitor to match the input, then hold it steady during the conversion
To store the final number
To pick which channel to read
items.explanation: The sample-and-hold captures the input on a small capacitor and freezes it so the converter has a stable value to work on. It does not amplify or store the result number.

items.prompt: A sensor with high source impedance gives readings that drift toward the previously measured channel. What is the best first fix?
choices: Lower the reference voltage
Increase the ADC sampling time, or add a buffer between the sensor and the pin
Switch from 12-bit to 8-bit
Turn on continuous mode
items.explanation: A weak (high-impedance) source charges the sample capacitor slowly, so a longer sampling time or a buffer lets it settle fully. Changing Vref, resolution, or mode does not address the charging problem.

items.prompt: What does "continuous" conversion mode mean?
choices: It converts once and then stops until triggered again
It starts a new conversion the moment the previous one finishes, repeating on its own
It requires a timer event for every single conversion
It reads every channel exactly once and stops
items.explanation: Continuous mode re-arms itself automatically and free-runs back-to-back. Single mode stops after one conversion; scan mode walks a channel list.

items.prompt: You need to read four different sensors, one after another, in a single sweep. Which mode is designed for that?
choices: Single mode
Scan / sequence mode
Software-trigger mode only
DAC mode
items.explanation: Scan (sequence) mode steps through an ordered list of channels, reading several sensors per sweep. Single mode does one conversion per trigger.

items.prompt: What does a sensor's source impedance describe, in plain terms?
choices: How bright the sensor's LED is
How easily the sensor can deliver charge to the ADC, roughly how 'stiff' or 'weak' its output is
The sensor's color
The number of bits in the ADC
items.explanation: Source impedance reflects how readily the source supplies charge. A high (weak) impedance fills the sample capacitor slowly, needing more sampling time.

items.prompt: Setting the sampling time too short results in what kind of error?
choices: The reference voltage drops
The sample capacitor has not fully charged, so the reading is pulled toward the previous value
The ADC switches to 8-bit automatically
The conversion never finishes
items.explanation: If the hold capacitor has not settled, leftover charge from the prior reading biases the result. Sampling time does not change Vref or resolution.

items.prompt: Which trigger source gives the most precise, even spacing between samples?
choices: A software 'start' bit set inside a polling loop
A hardware timer firing each conversion
Pressing a button
Whenever an interrupt happens to occur
items.explanation: A hardware timer produces a uniform, low-jitter sample interval. Software bits, button presses, and incidental interrupts all introduce uneven timing.

lessons.title: Timer to ADC to DMA, Oversampling, and Calibration
lessons.content:
## Hook

Suppose you want thousands of evenly spaced samples per second. Reading each one in code would swamp the CPU. There is a hands-off way: let hardware do the whole loop.

## Build

The pattern is **timer to ADC to DMA**. You met timers in an earlier sub-topic; **DMA** (direct memory access) is a helper that copies data without the CPU, covered more in the coming DMA sub-topic. Here is the chain:
1. A **timer** fires at your chosen rate and triggers each conversion.
2. The **ADC** converts and produces a result.
3. The **DMA** copies that result straight into a buffer (an array in RAM), all by itself.

The result is an evenly sampled block of data in memory with the CPU barely involved, ready for later processing. Often the buffer is a **circular buffer** with a half-done and a fully-done signal, so the CPU can work on one half while the other fills.

**Oversampling** is a trick to gain extra bits. If you take many readings of a slowly changing signal (which carries a little natural noise) and average them, the average is steadier than any single reading. Averaging $M$ samples roughly adds $\tfrac{1}{2}\log_2 M$ bits: averaging 256 readings buys about 4 extra bits of effective resolution. Some MCUs do this automatically in hardware.

**Calibration** corrects small built-in errors. **Offset** error is a constant shift (zero does not read as zero); **gain** error is a wrong slope (full-scale is slightly off). Many MCUs run a quick self-calibration at startup to reduce both.

## Recap

A timer-ADC-DMA chain captures an evenly sampled buffer with almost no CPU work; oversampling averages readings for extra effective bits; calibration trims offset and gain errors.

items.prompt: In the timer to ADC to DMA chain, what is each part's job?
choices: The timer stores data, the ADC triggers, the DMA converts
The timer triggers conversions at a fixed rate, the ADC converts, and the DMA copies results into a buffer without the CPU
The DMA triggers, the timer converts, the ADC stores
All three jobs are done by the CPU one at a time
items.explanation: Timer = the even clock that triggers; ADC = the converter; DMA = the mover that copies results to RAM. The whole point is to keep the CPU out of the per-sample loop.

items.prompt: What is the main benefit of using DMA to collect ADC samples?
choices: It increases the reference voltage
The CPU does not have to read each sample by hand, so it can do other work or stay idle
It makes the ADC use fewer bits
It removes the need for a timer
items.explanation: DMA copies each result automatically, freeing the CPU from per-sample reads. It does not change Vref or resolution, and a timer is still what sets the sample rate.

items.prompt: Why is a circular buffer with half-done and full-done signals useful for streaming ADC data?
choices: It doubles the sample rate
The CPU can process the half that just filled while the DMA keeps writing the other half, avoiding overwrites
It calibrates the offset error
It triggers the timer
items.explanation: The half/full signals create a ping-pong: process one half while the other fills, so data is never overwritten before it is used. It does not change the sample rate.

items.prompt: Oversampling by averaging 256 readings gives roughly how many extra effective bits?
choices: About 1 extra bit
About 2 extra bits
About 4 extra bits
About 8 extra bits
items.explanation: Extra bits is about (1/2) x log2(M). For M = 256, log2(256) = 8, so (1/2) x 8 = 4 extra bits.

items.prompt: Why does averaging many readings give a steadier result than a single reading?
choices: Averaging increases the reference voltage
Each reading carries a little random noise, and averaging makes that noise partly cancel out
Averaging changes the sensor's voltage
It only works if the ADC is 8-bit
items.explanation: Random noise tends to cancel when many samples are averaged, so the average sits closer to the true value. It is a software/hardware trick, not a change to Vref or the sensor.

items.prompt: What does offset error mean in an ADC?
choices: The full-scale reading has the wrong slope
A constant shift, so an input of zero does not read as exactly zero
The ADC is too slow
The reference voltage is missing
items.explanation: Offset is a constant shift of the whole scale (zero does not read zero). A wrong slope at full scale is gain error, a different thing.

items.prompt: What does calibration do for an ADC?
choices: It increases the number of bits permanently
It corrects built-in errors such as offset (zero shift) and gain (slope error)
It replaces the timer
It converts the result to text
items.explanation: Calibration trims offset and gain errors so readings are more accurate; many MCUs self-calibrate at startup. It does not add bits or replace other peripherals.

items.prompt: Oversampling trades what for the extra resolution it gives?
choices: It costs nothing at all
It takes more time / more samples, so it is slower per final reading
It needs a higher reference voltage
It needs a bigger DAC
items.explanation: You must take and combine many samples, so each final high-resolution value takes longer to produce. That speed-for-bits trade is the catch.

lessons.title: DAC Output, Waveforms with Timer + DMA, and ADC Types
lessons.content:
## Hook

Now flip the direction. Instead of reading the world, your program *creates* a voltage, and even a smooth, repeating waveform like a sine wave, with the same timer and DMA tools.

## Build

The **DAC** turns a number into a voltage on a pin. With the same convention as the ADC:

$$V_{out} = \frac{\text{count}}{2^N} \times V_{ref}$$

So on a 3.3 V, 12-bit DAC, writing 4095 outputs about 3.3 V; writing 2048 outputs about 1.65 V. Many DACs include an **output buffer** so they can drive a small load without sagging.

To make a **waveform**, you do not write each value by hand. You store one cycle of the shape (say, a sine) as a list of numbers in a table, then use a **timer + DMA** to feed those numbers to the DAC one after another at a steady rate. This is the same hands-off idea as the ADC chain, just reversed: timer sets the pace, DMA streams the table, DAC outputs the voltage.

This simple technique is called **DDS** (direct digital synthesis). The output frequency is:

$$f_{out} = \frac{f_{update}}{L}$$

where $f_{update}$ is how often you push a new value and $L$ is the table length. One full trip through the table is one cycle of the wave. A table of 100 entries updated at 1 MHz gives $1{,}000{,}000 / 100 = 10\,\text{kHz}$.

Finally, two common ADC styles, just conceptually. **SAR** (successive-approximation) ADCs are fast and give one result per conversion, great for general sensing and timer-triggered sampling. **Sigma-delta** ADCs oversample heavily to reach very high resolution, but are slower, great for audio or precise slow measurements.

## Recap

The DAC sets a voltage from a number; a timer + DMA streaming a table makes waveforms (DDS) with $f_{out} = f_{update}/L$; SAR is fast and general, sigma-delta is high-resolution but slower.

items.prompt: On a 12-bit, 3.3 V DAC, what voltage does writing a count of 2048 produce?
choices: About 0.81 mV
About 1.65 V
About 3.3 V
About 0 V
items.explanation: Vout = (count/4096) x Vref = (2048/4096) x 3.3 = 1.65 V, half of Vref. The DAC uses the same scale idea as the ADC.

items.prompt: To play a sine wave out of a DAC without the CPU writing each value by hand, you use:
choices: A single software write repeated forever
A timer + DMA streaming a table of values to the DAC at a steady rate
The ADC in scan mode
A bigger reference voltage
items.explanation: A timer paces the updates and DMA streams the lookup table into the DAC, the reverse of the ADC capture chain. This frees the CPU and keeps the timing even.

items.prompt: A DAC sine table has 100 entries and is updated by a timer at 1 MHz via DMA. What is the output frequency?
choices: 100 Hz
1 kHz
10 kHz
1 MHz
items.explanation: f_out = f_update / L = 1,000,000 / 100 = 10 kHz. One full pass through the 100-entry table is one cycle of the wave.

items.prompt: In the DDS formula f_out = f_update / L, what does L stand for?
choices: The reference voltage
The number of entries in the waveform table (one full pass = one cycle)
The number of ADC channels
The latency of the DAC
items.explanation: L is the table length. Stepping through all L entries once produces one cycle, so a longer table at the same update rate gives a lower output frequency.

items.prompt: What is the purpose of a DAC's output buffer?
choices: To increase the resolution
To let the output drive a small load without the voltage sagging
To convert voltage back into a number
To store the waveform table
items.explanation: A buffer provides drive strength so a modest load does not pull the output voltage down. It does not change resolution or store data.

items.prompt: Which ADC type is fast and gives one result per conversion, making it a good general choice for timer-triggered sensing?
choices: Sigma-delta
SAR (successive-approximation)
DAC
Open-drain
items.explanation: SAR ADCs convert quickly with low latency, one clean result per trigger, ideal for timer-driven sampling. Sigma-delta trades speed for very high resolution.

items.prompt: Which ADC type oversamples heavily to reach very high resolution but is slower, suiting audio or precise slow measurements?
choices: SAR
Sigma-delta
Flash only
DAC
items.explanation: Sigma-delta ADCs trade speed for resolution by oversampling, which fits audio and precise DC measurements. SAR is the faster, general-purpose choice.

items.prompt: You want a lower-frequency tone from the same DAC table without changing how fast you push values. What can you do?
choices: Use a shorter table
Use a longer table, since f_out = f_update / L drops as L grows
Raise the reference voltage
Switch to the ADC
items.explanation: With f_update fixed, increasing the table length L lowers f_out because one cycle now takes more updates. A shorter table would raise the frequency.

subtopics.title: Serial Protocols: UART, SPI, I2C, CAN

lessons.title: UART/USART: Asynchronous Framing, Baud, and Flow Control
lessons.content:
Chips need to talk: a microcontroller (the small computer on a circuit board) sends data to sensors, displays, and other chips. The simplest way is to send the bits one at a time over a single wire. That is what "serial" means. The opposite, "parallel," uses one wire per bit so a whole byte arrives at once -- fast, but it eats up many pins. Serial trades a little speed for far fewer wires, which is why almost everything uses it.

**UART** (Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter) is the oldest and simplest serial method. Think of a **walkie-talkie**: two people talk over one channel, and it only works if both set their radios to the same agreed setting beforehand. For UART that setting is the **baud rate** -- the number of bits sent per second (for example 9600 or 115200). It uses two data wires: **TX** (transmit, the talking wire) and **RX** (receive, the listening wire). One chip's TX connects to the other's RX.

UART is **asynchronous**, which means there is no separate clock wire telling the receiver when each bit arrives. Instead both sides already agreed on the baud rate, so the receiver knows how long each bit lasts. The wire rests HIGH when idle. A message (a "frame") begins with one **start bit** (a LOW pulse) that wakes the receiver, then the **data bits** (usually 8), then 1 or 2 **stop bits** (HIGH). The popular shorthand **8N1** means 8 data bits, No parity, 1 stop bit. The receiver sees the start bit fall, then samples each following bit in the middle of its time slot.

If one side talks faster than the other can process, bytes can be lost. **Flow control** prevents that: with hardware RTS/CTS lines, the receiver can signal "pause" until it catches up. UART itself is just the logic-level signaling (0 and 3.3V); to push it over long cables people use **RS-232** (higher voltages, point-to-point) or **RS-485** (a noise-resistant differential pair for long runs and many devices) -- same framing, different wires.

Recap: UART = a simple two-wire "direct phone line" where both ends agree on a speed (baud) and frame each byte with start and stop bits.

items.prompt: Look at the diagram of a UART frame. The TX line rests HIGH when idle, then drops LOW for one bit. What is that first LOW bit called, and what is its job?
choices: The stop bit; it ends the previous message
The start bit; it tells the receiver a new frame is beginning so it can sync up
The parity bit; it checks for errors
The clock bit; it carries the shared timing signal
items.explanation: The frame begins with a single LOW start bit. Because UART has no clock wire, the receiver watches for this HIGH-to-LOW edge to know exactly when a frame begins, then times the following bits using the agreed baud rate. The stop bit comes at the end (HIGH), and parity (if used) sits between data and stop.

items.prompt: What is the difference between "serial" and "parallel" data transfer?
choices: Serial sends all bits at once on many wires; parallel sends one bit at a time
Serial sends bits one at a time over few wires; parallel sends many bits at once over many wires
Serial is only for displays; parallel is only for sensors
They are two names for the exact same thing
items.explanation: Serial means bits travel one after another over just a wire or two, saving pins. Parallel uses one wire per bit so a whole byte moves at once -- faster but pin-hungry. UART, SPI, I2C, and CAN are all serial protocols.

items.prompt: Why does UART not need a separate clock wire?
choices: Because it sends bits in parallel, so timing does not matter
Because both sides agreed on the same baud rate in advance, so the receiver knows how long each bit lasts
Because the start bit contains the clock frequency encoded inside it
Because UART can only send one byte ever, so timing is irrelevant
items.explanation: UART is asynchronous: there is no shared clock line. Both ends are preconfigured to the same baud rate (bits per second), so once the receiver sees the start bit it can time every following bit on its own. This is why a wrong baud setting produces garbage.

items.prompt: The shorthand "8N1" describes a UART link. What does it mean?
choices: 8 stop bits, No data, 1 parity bit
8 data bits, No parity, 1 stop bit
8 wires, No clock, 1 device
8000 baud, No flow control, 1 byte buffer
items.explanation: 8N1 = 8 data bits, No parity, 1 stop bit. It is the most common UART setting. (There is also always an implicit start bit, and the line idles HIGH between frames.) Both ends must use the same framing or they will misread each other.

items.prompt: What are the TX and RX wires used for in a UART connection?
choices: TX is the clock; RX is the chip-select
TX transmits data out; RX receives data in. One chip's TX connects to the other chip's RX
TX and RX are both ground wires
TX is power; RX is the data line
items.explanation: TX = transmit (the talking wire), RX = receive (the listening wire). For two chips to talk, TX on one goes to RX on the other and vice versa. A common beginner mistake is wiring TX-to-TX, which means neither side ever hears the other.

items.prompt: Two devices are connected by UART. One is set to 9600 baud and the other to 115200 baud. What happens?
choices: It works fine; baud rates auto-negotiate
The receiver mistimes every bit and reads garbage, because the bit durations no longer match
Only the faster device can send; the slower one is muted
The data is correct but arrives twice as fast
items.explanation: Because UART has no clock wire, both ends MUST agree on the baud rate. If they disagree, the receiver samples at the wrong moments and decodes nonsense. Matching the baud rate on both sides is the first thing to check when a UART link prints garbage.

items.prompt: What problem does hardware flow control (RTS/CTS) solve?
choices: It encrypts the data on the wire
It lets a busy receiver tell the sender to pause, so bytes are not lost when the receiver cannot keep up
It increases the baud rate automatically
It adds a clock wire to make UART synchronous
items.explanation: If a sender talks faster than the receiver can process, the receiver's small buffer overflows and bytes vanish silently. Flow control adds signal lines (RTS/CTS) so the receiver can say "pause" until it catches up. It does not change speed or add a clock.

items.prompt: How are UART and RS-485 related?
choices: RS-485 replaces UART framing with a completely different, clocked scheme
They are unrelated; RS-485 is a type of WiFi
UART is the data framing (start/data/stop bits); RS-485 is just a tougher set of wires/voltages that can carry that same framing over long, noisy distances
RS-485 is slower than UART because it uses more wires
items.explanation: UART describes the logic-level framing of the bytes. RS-232 and RS-485 are physical layers -- different voltages and wiring -- that carry the same async framing. RS-485 uses a differential pair (two wires with opposite signals) so it survives long runs and electrical noise, and it can connect many devices.

items.prompt: Which everyday job is UART a natural fit for?
choices: Driving a high-resolution color display at 60 frames per second
A simple point-to-point link like a debug console, a GPS module, or a Bluetooth/cellular modem
Connecting 50 temperature sensors to one chip using as few wires as possible
A rugged network inside a car that survives heavy electrical noise
items.explanation: UART shines for simple two-device links: a serial console, a GPS receiver, or a modem. High-speed displays favor SPI; many low-speed sensors on few wires favor I2C; a noisy automotive network favors CAN. Matching the bus to the job is a recurring theme.

items.prompt: After the data bits in an 8N1 frame, the line goes HIGH for the stop bit and stays HIGH. Why does the idle line rest HIGH?
choices: So the next start bit's HIGH-to-LOW drop is a clear, detectable edge that signals a new frame
Because HIGH uses less power than LOW
Because the stop bit is actually the clock
Because a HIGH line means the cable is disconnected
items.explanation: The idle and stop levels are HIGH precisely so the next frame's start bit (a drop to LOW) stands out as a clean falling edge the receiver can detect. If the line idled LOW, the receiver could not tell idle from a start bit.

lessons.title: SPI: Full-Duplex, Clock + Chip Select, and Why It Is Fast
lessons.content:
**SPI** (Serial Peripheral Interface) is the fast cousin of UART. Picture a **conveyor belt synced by a metronome**: a steady beat keeps both sides perfectly in step, so data can fly by very quickly. That metronome is a real wire called the **clock** (SCLK), and it is what makes SPI **synchronous** -- unlike UART, there is no guessing about timing, because the clock literally tells the other side when to read each bit.

SPI has one boss, the **master** (usually the microcontroller), and one or more **devices** (sensors, displays, memory chips). It uses these lines:

- **SCLK** -- the clock wire (the metronome), driven by the master.
- **MOSI** -- Master Out, device In: data the master sends.
- **MISO** -- Master In, device Out: data the device sends back.
- **CS** (chip-select, sometimes SS) -- one per device, usually active-LOW.

Because there are separate wires for sending (MOSI) and receiving (MISO), data flows **both ways at the same time** on every clock tick. That two-way-at-once behavior is called **full-duplex**.

The **chip-select** is how the master picks which device to talk to. SCLK, MOSI, and MISO are shared by all devices, but each device gets its own CS line. To talk to a device, the master pulls that device's CS LOW; the others ignore the bus. This is the key SPI rule: **SPI needs a separate chip-select line per device.** That is also why SPI is called "pin-hungry" -- three shared wires plus one extra pin for every device you add.

SPI's upside is raw speed (tens of millions of bits per second, no waiting) and simplicity (no addresses, no acknowledgments). Its downside is the pin count and the lack of any built-in error signal -- if something is wrong, you just read wrong data with no warning.

There is one timing detail to match: the master and device must agree on which clock edge reads the data (this is set by two settings called clock polarity and clock phase, giving four "modes" 0-3). If they disagree, every byte comes out as garbage with no error flag, so you check the device's datasheet for the right mode.

Recap: SPI = a fast, clock-synced bus with separate send/receive wires (full-duplex) and one chip-select per device.

items.prompt: Study the SPI wiring diagram: one master connects to two devices. SCLK, MOSI, and MISO are shared, but each device has its own CS line. How does the master choose which device to talk to?
choices: It sends the device's address on the MOSI line
It pulls that device's chip-select (CS) line LOW, while the other devices ignore the bus
It changes the clock frequency to match the device
It uses a different MOSI wire for each device
items.explanation: SPI selects a device by pulling its dedicated CS line LOW (active-low). Only the selected device responds; the rest stay quiet. SPI has no addressing scheme (that is I2C), which is exactly why it needs one CS pin per device.

items.prompt: What makes SPI a synchronous bus, and what is the benefit?
choices: It has a dedicated clock wire (SCLK) driven by the master, so both sides stay in step and can go very fast
Both sides guess the timing from an agreed baud rate
It synchronizes using the start and stop bits in each frame
It uses the chip-select line as a clock
items.explanation: SPI is synchronous because the master drives a real clock wire, SCLK, like a metronome. Each tick shifts one bit, so there is no timing guesswork and the bus can run very fast. UART, by contrast, is asynchronous and relies on a pre-agreed baud rate.

items.prompt: What do MOSI and MISO carry in an SPI link?
choices: MOSI is the clock; MISO is the chip-select
MOSI carries data from the master to the device; MISO carries data from the device back to the master
They are two redundant ground wires
MOSI is power out; MISO is power in
items.explanation: MOSI = Master Out, Slave/device In. MISO = Master In, Slave/device Out. Having separate wires for each direction is what lets SPI send and receive at the same time (full-duplex). SCLK is the clock and CS is the chip-select.

items.prompt: SPI is described as "full-duplex." What does that mean?
choices: It can connect to a maximum of two devices
Data flows in both directions at the same time, because there are separate send (MOSI) and receive (MISO) wires
It sends each byte twice for reliability
It only works when both devices are powered fully
items.explanation: Full-duplex means simultaneous two-way traffic. On every clock tick the master shifts a bit out on MOSI and shifts a bit in on MISO at the same instant. Separate wires for each direction make this possible; UART and I2C are not full-duplex in this way.

items.prompt: You want to connect three SPI devices to one microcontroller. How many chip-select lines do you need, and why?
choices: One CS total, shared by all three devices
Three CS lines, one per device, because SPI selects a device by pulling its own CS LOW
Zero, because SPI uses addresses instead of chip-selects
Six, two per device
items.explanation: SPI has no addressing, so it picks a device using a dedicated chip-select. Three devices need three CS pins (plus the shared SCLK/MOSI/MISO). This per-device pin cost is why SPI is called pin-hungry. (Daisy-chaining can share one CS, but only with devices that support it.)

items.prompt: Why is SPI considered "pin-hungry" compared to I2C?
choices: Because it needs a power wire for every device
Because beyond the three shared wires it needs an extra chip-select pin for each device, while I2C uses just two shared wires for everyone
Because SPI uses 16-bit addresses
Because SPI requires pull-up resistors on every line
items.explanation: SPI shares SCLK/MOSI/MISO but adds one CS pin per device, so wiring grows with each device. I2C keeps just two shared wires (SDA/SCL) no matter how many devices, using addresses instead of select lines. That is the classic pins-versus-speed trade between them.

items.prompt: You bring up a new SPI sensor. The clock and chip-select look correct on a logic analyzer, but the sensor's ID register reads back as all zeros instead of the expected value. What is the most likely cause?
choices: Missing pull-up resistors on MOSI and MISO
The clock polarity/phase (SPI mode) does not match the sensor's datasheet, so bits are sampled on the wrong edge and come out as garbage
The baud rate is set too low
The sensor needs its own address assigned
items.explanation: Clean clocking but a wrong/zeroed read is the classic SPI mode mismatch: master and device disagree on which clock edge reads the data, so every bit is captured at the wrong moment. SPI gives no error flag for this -- you fix it by matching the mode from the datasheet. Pull-ups and addresses are I2C concerns.

items.prompt: An SPI link reads wrong data, yet there is no error or warning bit anywhere. Why does SPI not warn you?
choices: Because SPI has no built-in acknowledgment or error-check mechanism; it just shifts bits with no handshake
Because the master disabled error reporting by default
Because the chip-select line suppresses error flags
Because errors are only reported over a separate UART
items.explanation: SPI is deliberately bare: it shifts bits in and out with no addressing, acknowledgment, or error bit. That simplicity is part of why it is fast, but it means problems like a mode mismatch show up only as wrong data. You debug with a logic analyzer and the datasheet.

items.prompt: For which job is SPI the best fit among the four buses?
choices: A simple two-wire debug console to a PC
Streaming a lot of data fast to a nearby device, like refreshing a display or reading a flash memory chip
Connecting 30 slow sensors with the fewest possible wires
A rugged car network that must survive heavy electrical noise over long cables
items.explanation: SPI's strength is raw speed over short distances, making it ideal for displays, flash/SD storage, and fast sensors. A simple console is UART's job; many slow sensors on few wires is I2C; a noisy long-distance network is CAN.

lessons.title: I2C: Two Shared Wires, Addresses, ACK, and Pull-Ups
lessons.content:
**I2C** (Inter-Integrated Circuit, said "I-squared-C") solves a different problem than SPI: how do you connect lots of devices using as few wires as possible? Its trick is to use just **two shared wires** for everything:

- **SDA** -- the data line.
- **SCL** -- the clock line.

Every device hangs off the same two wires. So how does the master reach just one of them? Think of an old **telephone party line** where everyone shares one line and you call a specific person by name. In I2C, each device has its own **address** (a small number, usually 7 bits). The master announces an address on the bus; only the matching device responds.

Here is the part beginners must not miss. The two wires are **open-drain**, which means any device can pull a wire LOW, but nobody actively drives it HIGH. To return to HIGH, the wires rely on two small **pull-up resistors** (commonly around 4.7k ohms) connecting SDA and SCL up to the supply voltage. This is the most important I2C rule: **I2C needs pull-up resistors on SDA and SCL.** Without them the lines can never reach a valid HIGH, and the bus simply does not work -- no device ever answers. (This is exactly the opposite of SPI, whose rule is one chip-select per device.)

A message works like this: the master sends a **START** signal, then the device's **address** plus a bit saying read or write. The addressed device replies with an **ACK** (acknowledge) by briefly pulling SDA LOW -- its way of saying "that's me, I'm here." If no device answers, that is a **NACK** (no acknowledge). Data bytes follow, each also acknowledged, and the master ends with a **STOP** signal.

Two more handy ideas: a slow device can hold the clock line LOW to say "wait, I need a moment" -- this is called **clock stretching**, and the master must pause for it. And if two masters talk at once, they sort out who goes first without corrupting data (arbitration), thanks to the open-drain wired behavior.

The trade-off versus SPI: I2C uses far fewer wires and supports many devices, but it is slower and has more rules. 

Recap: I2C = two shared wires, each device addressed by name, with pull-up resistors providing the HIGH and an ACK confirming each byte.

items.prompt: Look at the I2C bus diagram: two pull-up resistors connect SDA and SCL to the supply, and several addressed devices share those two wires. What happens if you forget the pull-up resistors?
choices: The bus runs twice as fast
The lines can never reach a valid HIGH, so no device ever responds and the bus does not work
Only the first device works; the rest are disabled
The data is fine but the clock stops
items.explanation: I2C lines are open-drain: devices can only pull LOW, so the pull-up resistors are what create the HIGH level. With no pull-ups the lines sit stuck low, the master can never even generate a proper START, and every transfer fails. Missing pull-ups is the number-one I2C mistake.

items.prompt: How many signal wires does I2C use, and what are they?
choices: Three: SCLK, MOSI, and MISO
Two: SDA (data) and SCL (clock), shared by every device
Four: TX, RX, RTS, and CTS
One: a single combined data-and-clock wire
items.explanation: I2C uses just two shared wires, SDA (data) and SCL (clock). Every device connects to those same two lines, which is what lets many devices share very few pins. SCLK/MOSI/MISO are SPI; TX/RX are UART.

items.prompt: With many devices on the same two wires, how does the I2C master reach just one specific device?
choices: It pulls that device's chip-select line LOW
It sends the device's unique address on the bus; only the matching device responds
It changes the clock speed to match the device
It is impossible; I2C can only have one device
items.explanation: I2C is like a party line: each device has its own address, and the master announces an address so only that device answers. This addressing is why I2C does not need a select wire per device the way SPI does.

items.prompt: Why does I2C need external pull-up resistors on SDA and SCL?
choices: To make the bus run faster
Because the lines are open-drain: devices can only pull LOW, so the resistors are what pull the lines back up to HIGH
To protect the devices from too much current
Because every device must have its own resistor to get an address
items.explanation: Open-drain means a device can sink a line to LOW but cannot source it to HIGH. The pull-up resistors provide the HIGH level. This shared scheme lets many devices share the wires without fighting each other, but it means no pull-ups equals no working bus.

items.prompt: On I2C, after the master sends a device's address, the addressed device briefly pulls SDA LOW. What is this called and what does it mean?
choices: A NACK, meaning the device is busy
An ACK (acknowledge), the device's way of saying "that's my address, I'm here"
A STOP condition, ending the message
Clock stretching, asking the master to slow down
items.explanation: Pulling SDA LOW at the acknowledge moment is an ACK: the addressed device confirms it received the byte and is present. If no device pulls it low (it floats high), that is a NACK, meaning no one answered or the device cannot accept more.

items.prompt: What is I2C clock stretching, and who does it?
choices: The master speeds up the clock to push a slow device
A device holds the SCL clock line LOW to pause the master until it is ready; the master must wait
The master stretches the data line to send bigger bytes
Two devices share one clock to save power
items.explanation: Clock stretching is a device's way of asking for time: it holds SCL LOW so the master cannot start the next clock pulse until the device releases it. A correct master waits for SCL to actually rise. It is a device-side pause, not a master action.

items.prompt: You wire up a new I2C sensor, but scanning for it finds nothing, and a scope shows SDA and SCL sitting near 0V with no clean HIGH. What is the most likely cause?
choices: A clock polarity/phase mode mismatch
Missing or far-too-weak pull-up resistors on SDA and SCL
The chip-select line has the wrong polarity
The baud rate is set too high
items.explanation: Lines that never reach a clean HIGH (stuck low, rounded edges, nothing responding) are the signature of missing or insufficient pull-ups, since open-drain lines have nothing else to source the HIGH. Mode mismatch and chip-select are SPI concepts; baud rate is UART.

items.prompt: Which statement best captures the trade-off between I2C and SPI?
choices: I2C is faster and uses more wires; SPI is slower and uses fewer wires
I2C uses fewer wires and supports many addressed devices but is slower; SPI is faster but needs a chip-select pin per device
They are identical except for the connector shape
SPI uses addresses while I2C uses chip-selects
items.explanation: I2C wins on wire count and device count (two shared wires, addressing) at the cost of speed and extra rules like pull-ups and ACKs. SPI wins on speed and simplicity but spends a chip-select pin per device. Remember: I2C needs pull-ups; SPI needs a CS per device.

items.prompt: What does a START condition followed by an address tell the devices on an I2C bus?
choices: That the bus is being powered off
That a new message is beginning and which device it is meant for; only the device with that address will respond
That every device should reply at once
That the master is switching to SPI mode
items.explanation: The START signal wakes the bus for a new transaction, and the address right after it names the target device. All devices hear the address, but only the one whose address matches will ACK and take part. A STOP later ends the transaction.

items.prompt: For which job is I2C the most natural choice?
choices: Streaming a full-screen video to a display at high speed
Connecting several low-speed on-board sensors (temperature, humidity, a small OLED) using as few wires as possible
A simple two-wire console link to a laptop
A 40-meter noisy industrial network with priority messages
items.explanation: I2C is ideal for several modest-speed chips on the same board where saving pins matters. Fast displays favor SPI, a console favors UART, and a long noisy network with priorities favors CAN. Picking the right bus for the job is the recurring skill.

lessons.title: CAN, and Choosing a Bus + Transfer Mode
lessons.content:
**CAN** (Controller Area Network) is built for tough, noisy places -- it is literally **the network inside your car**, and it is common in factories and machines too. Where UART, SPI, and I2C are happy on a quiet circuit board, CAN is designed to survive long cables and heavy electrical noise from motors and ignition systems.

Its secret is **differential** signaling. Instead of one wire carrying a voltage measured against ground, CAN uses **two wires, CANH and CANL**, that carry opposite signals. A receiver looks at the *difference* between them. When electrical noise hits the cable, it hits both wires almost equally, so the difference stays clean and the noise cancels out. A small chip called a **transceiver** converts the microcontroller's signals to and from this two-wire pair. Both ends of the cable get a **120-ohm termination resistor** to absorb the signal and prevent echoes (reflections) that would corrupt data.

CAN is **message-based, not address-based**. A message does not say "to device 5"; instead it carries an **ID** describing what the message *is* (for example "engine RPM"). Every node hears every message and keeps the ones it cares about. That ID also sets **priority**: if two nodes try to talk at once, the one with the lower-numbered ID automatically wins, and -- crucially -- the winner's message is not damaged at all. This non-destructive **priority arbitration** guarantees that urgent messages (like brakes) always get through first. **CAN FD** is a newer version that carries more data per message and runs faster.

**Choosing among the four buses** comes down to matching the job:

- **UART** -- a simple point-to-point link (console, GPS, modem).
- **SPI** -- fastest, short distance, few devices (displays, flash).
- **I2C** -- many low-speed devices, fewest pins, on one board.
- **CAN** -- many devices over long, noisy distances with message priority (cars, industrial).

Finally, no matter which bus you use, the chip can move the data three ways. **Polled**: the processor keeps checking "any data yet?" in a loop -- simplest, but wastes effort. **Interrupt-driven**: the hardware taps the processor on the shoulder only when something arrives -- a good general default. **DMA** (Direct Memory Access): a helper engine moves blocks of data straight to memory without bothering the processor at all -- best for high speed or large transfers, because the processor stays free.

Recap: CAN = a rugged differential bus where messages carry IDs that set priority, ideal for noisy multi-device networks; and you move data by polling, interrupts, or DMA depending on how busy you want the processor to be.

items.prompt: Study the CAN bus diagram: two wires (CANH and CANL) carry opposite signals, with a 120-ohm resistor at each end and several nodes attached. Why does CAN use two wires with opposite signals (differential signaling)?
choices: To double the data rate
So that electrical noise hits both wires equally and cancels out when the receiver looks at the difference, making the bus noise-resistant
So each wire can carry a different message at once
Because one wire is for power and one is for data
items.explanation: Differential signaling means the receiver reads the difference between CANH and CANL. Noise picked up along the cable affects both wires almost identically, so it cancels in the difference. That is why CAN survives the harsh electrical environment of a car or factory.

items.prompt: Where is CAN most commonly used, and why is it suited to that environment?
choices: Inside a single quiet circuit board, because it is the simplest bus
In cars and industrial machines, because its differential wiring resists electrical noise over long cables
Only between a PC and a single sensor, like a debug console
For the fastest possible short-distance memory transfers
items.explanation: CAN was designed for vehicles and industrial systems: many nodes, long noisy cabling, and a need for reliable real-time messaging. Its differential signaling and error handling make it rugged. On a quiet board you would more likely use I2C or SPI.

items.prompt: How is CAN addressing different from I2C addressing?
choices: CAN messages carry an ID describing the message itself, not a destination device; every node hears every message and keeps the ones it wants
CAN assigns each device a chip-select line instead of an address
CAN has no IDs at all; messages are anonymous
CAN addresses are exactly the same as I2C 7-bit addresses
items.explanation: CAN is message-oriented: the ID labels the content ("engine RPM"), not a recipient. Every node receives every frame and filters for what it cares about. I2C, by contrast, addresses a specific device. This is a fundamental difference in how the two buses think.

items.prompt: Two CAN nodes start sending at the same instant: one message has ID 0x080, the other has ID 0x100. What happens?
choices: Both messages collide and are destroyed; both retry randomly
The lower ID (0x080) wins automatically and sends without damage; the other node backs off and retries when the bus is free
The higher ID (0x100) wins because bigger numbers are higher priority
Both messages are sent at once on separate wires
items.explanation: CAN uses non-destructive priority arbitration: the lower-numbered ID has higher priority and wins, while its message stays completely intact. The losing node simply waits and retries. This guarantees urgent low-ID messages get through first with no delay -- unlike Ethernet, where collisions destroy data.

items.prompt: Why does a CAN bus need a 120-ohm resistor at each end?
choices: To limit the current so devices do not burn out
To absorb the signal at the ends of the cable and prevent reflections (echoes) that would corrupt data
To set each device's address
To boost the voltage for long cables
items.explanation: The 120-ohm terminators match the cable's characteristic impedance, soaking up the signal at each end so it does not bounce back as reflections. Missing or wrong termination causes ringing and bit errors, especially on long or fast buses.

items.prompt: What is the job of the CAN transceiver chip?
choices: It stores all the messages on the bus
