So you keep the total well under 100% and leave **headroom** — spare capacity that absorbs the rare slow moments (the worst-case tail). Running a chip at 100% load leaves nothing for the bad day.

**Two habits that quietly wreck the worst case** (and connect back to the enemies of determinism):

- **Asking the system for memory while it is running** ('dynamic memory' from a pool called the **heap**). The time this takes depends on the memory's current state, so it has no dependable worst case. Real-time designs prefer to set aside all their memory up front.
- **Loops with no limit.** A loop like 'keep going until some signal arrives', with nothing capping how many times it can run, has no worst case at all — it could run a few times or run forever. In a real-time path, every loop needs a bound.

**Recap:** design for the worst case, because a deadline is a promise about every run, not the average. Keep a CPU budget with headroom, and avoid the things — run-time memory requests and unbounded loops — that destroy a knowable worst case.

items.prompt: In real-time design, which case should you design around?
choices: The average (typical) case, since it happens most often
The worst case, because the deadline must be met on every run
The best case, to look as fast as possible
It does not matter which case you pick
items.explanation: A deadline is a promise about every single run, so you must design around the slowest possible run — the worst case. Optimizing the average leaves the rare slow runs unproven, and those are the ones that miss.

items.prompt: For a hard deadline of 50 microseconds, which implementation is the safest choice?
choices: Usually 5 µs, but sometimes 200 µs
Dependably 30 µs every time
Usually 2 µs, worst case never measured
Averages 8 µs but with no known upper limit
items.explanation: A steady 30 µs is comfortably under the 50 µs deadline with a known bound, so it meets the promise every time. The others either exceed the deadline in their worst case or have an unknown worst case, which is unacceptable for a hard deadline.

items.prompt: On the chart of many timing measurements, which part decides whether a hard deadline is met?
choices: The tall peak near the typical (average) value
The long tail of rare slow runs
The number of measurements taken
The color of the bars
items.explanation: The deadline must sit beyond the tail of slow runs, because those rare long runs are the ones that can miss. The tall average peak can look perfectly fine while the tail still violates the deadline.

items.prompt: What is a 'CPU budget' in real-time design?
choices: The money spent buying the processor
Adding up the slice of processor time each task uses to check it stays under 100%
The amount of memory each task uses
The clock speed of the CPU in megahertz
items.explanation: A CPU budget tallies how much of the available processor time all the tasks consume. If the total goes over 100%, the chip is overcommitted and a deadline will be missed. It is about time, not money, memory, or clock speed.

items.prompt: Why should you leave 'headroom' instead of loading the processor to 100%?
choices: To make the average case slower on purpose
To leave spare time that absorbs the rare slow (worst-case) moments
Because processors cannot physically reach 100%
To reduce the amount of memory used
items.explanation: Headroom is spare CPU time that covers the occasional worst-case spike, so deadlines still hold on a bad day. A chip pinned at 100% has nothing left to absorb those spikes.

items.prompt: What happens if the tasks' CPU usage adds up to more than 100% of the available time?
choices: Nothing, the CPU simply tries harder
The processor is overcommitted and at least one task will miss its deadline
The average latency improves
All deadlines become hard real-time
items.explanation: There is only so much processor time per second; demanding more than 100% means the work cannot all fit, so something must miss its deadline. The CPU cannot create extra time by trying harder.

items.prompt: Why is asking for memory at run time (dynamic allocation from the heap) discouraged in real-time code?
choices: It always runs out of memory immediately
The time it takes depends on the memory's current state, so it has no dependable worst case
It can only be done while interrupts are off
It makes the average case slower but the worst case faster
items.explanation: How long a run-time memory request takes varies with the state of the heap, giving it no reliable upper bound — which destroys the knowable worst case real-time needs. That is why real-time designs set memory aside up front instead.

items.prompt: Why does a loop with no limit on how many times it can run hurt determinism?
choices: It uses too much flash memory
It has no worst case — it could finish quickly or run essentially forever
It automatically turns off the CPU
Loops are never allowed in any program
items.explanation: If nothing caps the iterations, there is no upper bound on the loop's run time, so it has no worst case at all. Real-time paths require every loop to be bounded so its longest possible run is known.

items.prompt: How does the optimization goal differ between an ordinary desktop program and a hard real-time program?
choices: They are identical — both only care about the average
Desktop tunes the average and tolerates rare hiccups; hard real-time accepts a worse average to guarantee a bounded worst case
Hard real-time only cares about using the least memory
Desktop programs care only about the worst case
items.explanation: Desktop software optimizes the typical case and shrugs off the rare slow run, while hard real-time will accept a slightly slower average if that caps the worst case and protects every deadline. The priorities are essentially reversed.

items.prompt: A task averages 8 microseconds but its worst case has never been measured. Why is this risky for a hard deadline?
choices: Because 8 µs is too slow for any system
Because the deadline depends on the worst case, and an unknown worst case might exceed it
Because averages cannot be measured on real hardware
Because it means the task uses no CPU time
items.explanation: A hard deadline is judged by the slowest run, so an unmeasured worst case is an unknown that could quietly violate it. A good average gives false comfort — you must know and bound the worst case.

lessons.title: Why Embedded Debugging Is Different
lessons.content:
**Hook.** On your laptop, when a program misbehaves you add a `print` and read the message on the screen. A tiny microcontroller (MCU — the little computer chip inside a gadget) usually has *no screen at all*. So how do you see what your program and the hardware are doing? You need special tools.

**Build.** Think of a doctor. A patient says "I feel bad" — that is like "my gadget doesn't work." The doctor doesn't guess; they reach for a tool that reveals one specific thing: a stethoscope to hear the heart, a thermometer for temperature, an X-ray to see bones. Each tool answers a different question, and the doctor picks the right one for the symptom.

Embedded debugging works the same way. There are four everyday tools, each answering a different question:

- A **debugger** lets you pause your program and look inside it (what is my *code* doing?).
- A **logic analyzer** shows the 1s and 0s on several wires over time (what *bytes* are on the bus?).
- An **oscilloscope** shows the real voltage shape of a signal (what does the electrical signal actually *look* like?).
- A **GPIO toggle** flips an output pin so you can measure timing on a scope (how *long* did something take?).

The single most useful habit is a **hypothesis-test mindset**: instead of changing things at random, say out loud "I think the problem is X," then pick the one tool that would prove or disprove X, and look. If you were wrong, you've still learned something and you form a new hypothesis.

**Recap.** Embedded debugging is different because there is no screen, so you use external tools to *see* the program and the signals. Pick the tool that answers your current question, and always test a clear guess rather than poking blindly.

items.prompt: Study the diagram of the four tools. Which question is the *debugger* best suited to answer?
choices: What does the analog voltage shape look like?
What is my code doing right now (pause and inspect variables)?
How many milliamps is the board drawing?
What is the resistance of a pull-up resistor?
items.explanation: The debugger looks inside your running program — it pauses execution and lets you read variables. Signal shape is the oscilloscope's job; the debugger does not measure current or resistance.

items.prompt: Why can't you debug a typical bare-metal MCU the same easy way you debug a laptop program?
choices: MCUs run too fast for any tool to keep up
A typical MCU has no screen or console to print to, so you need external tools to see what is happening
MCUs don't run real code, only fixed firmware
Printing is illegal on microcontrollers
items.explanation: The core difference is the missing screen: there is usually nowhere to display a message, so you reach for a debugger, logic analyzer, scope, or pin toggle. MCUs do run real code, and speed is not the fundamental issue here.

items.prompt: The lesson compares embedded tools to a doctor's tools. What is the point of that comparison?
choices: All the tools do the same thing, so any one will do
Each tool reveals a different specific thing, so you choose the right one for the symptom
The most expensive tool is always the best choice
You should use every tool at once on every bug
items.explanation: A stethoscope, thermometer, and X-ray each answer a different question. Likewise each debug tool answers a different question — the skill is matching the tool to the symptom, not owning the priciest one.

items.prompt: What does a 'hypothesis-test' debugging mindset mean in practice?
choices: Change several things at once and see if anything improves
State a clear guess about the cause, then use one tool to prove or disprove it
Always assume the hardware is broken
Rewrite the whole program from scratch when stuck
items.explanation: You form a specific guess ("I think X is the cause") and run the one test that would confirm or rule it out. Changing many things at once teaches you nothing about which change mattered.

items.prompt: Your question is "did the chip actually send the right data bytes out on a communication wire?" Which tool fits best?
choices: Oscilloscope, to see the voltage shape
Logic analyzer, to read the 1s and 0s and decode them into bytes
Debugger, to single-step the code
A multimeter, to measure average voltage
items.explanation: Reading the actual bits/bytes on several wires over time is exactly what a logic analyzer does. The scope shows shape, not decoded bytes; the debugger shows code state, not the wire.

items.prompt: A wire's signal is 'sometimes misread' and you suspect the electrical shape is bad (not the data values). Which tool answers that?
choices: Logic analyzer, because it shows clean 1s and 0s
Oscilloscope, because it shows the real continuous voltage shape
Debugger, because it can pause the code
GPIO toggle, because it marks events
items.explanation: The oscilloscope shows the actual analog voltage over time, revealing slow edges, ringing, or glitches. A logic analyzer only reports 0/1 and would hide a bad shape.

items.prompt: You want to know how many microseconds a piece of code takes to run. What is a simple, low-cost way to measure it?
choices: Add a print statement and time it by eye
Flip a GPIO pin high at the start and low at the end, and measure the pulse on a scope
Guess based on the number of lines of code
Read the datasheet's typical figure and trust it
items.explanation: Toggling a pin costs almost nothing and the pulse width on a scope directly shows the duration. Printing is far too slow and imprecise for microsecond timing.

items.prompt: A gadget 'just doesn't work.' What is the best first move using this mindset?
choices: Swap random parts until it works
Narrow the problem to one clear guess, then pick the single tool that tests it
Add as many print statements as possible everywhere
Assume it is unfixable and start over
items.explanation: Start by forming a specific, testable hypothesis and choosing the matching tool. Random swaps and scattershot prints waste time and can introduce new problems.

items.prompt: Among the four tools, which one looks *inside your program* rather than at the wires?
choices: Oscilloscope
Logic analyzer
Debugger
GPIO toggle
items.explanation: The debugger inspects the program itself — its variables and where execution is. The other three observe electrical signals on the pins/wires.

lessons.title: The Debugger over SWD: Pause, Step, Watch
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Imagine you could freeze your program at any moment and look inside — see exactly which line it's on and what every variable holds. That is what a **debugger** does, and on a microcontroller it is often the very first tool you reach for.

**Build.** To talk to the chip, a small box called a **debug probe** connects your PC to the board using just two signal wires. This two-wire link is called **SWD** (Serial Wire Debug — a standard way to control an ARM chip). The probe can pause the chip, read its memory and variables, and let it run again, all without changing your code.

Three everyday debugger moves:

- **Breakpoint** = "stop here." You mark a line; when the program reaches it, the chip freezes so you can look around.
- **Step** = "run one line, then stop." You walk through the code one line at a time and watch what happens.
- **Watch a variable** = "show me this value." While paused, you read (or even change) any variable, like a temperature reading or a loop counter.

Why is this powerful for a beginner? Because instead of guessing, you can *see*. Hypothesis: "I think `count` never reaches 10." Set a breakpoint inside the loop, watch `count`, step a few times — now you know.

One caution: pausing the chip stops time for the *program*, but the outside world keeps going. If a motor was spinning or data was arriving, it won't wait for you. So the debugger is perfect for logic bugs ("my math is wrong") but can be misleading for timing bugs (covered later).

**Recap.** A debug probe over SWD lets you pause, step line-by-line, and watch variables on a chip with no screen. Use it to *see* your code's logic instead of guessing.

items.prompt: Study the SWD setup diagram. What sits between the PC and the target MCU?
choices: Nothing — the PC connects straight to the chip's pins
A debug probe, linked to the PC by USB and to the chip by the two SWD wires
An oscilloscope
A second microcontroller running the same program
items.explanation: The debug probe is the translator in the middle: USB to the PC on one side, the two SWD wires (SWDIO and SWCLK) to the target on the other. The PC does not drive the chip's debug pins directly.

items.prompt: What is SWD?
choices: A type of memory inside the MCU
A two-wire link (Serial Wire Debug) a probe uses to pause and inspect an ARM chip
A programming language for microcontrollers
A kind of oscilloscope probe
items.explanation: SWD (Serial Wire Debug) is the standard two-wire debug interface on ARM Cortex-M chips. It carries debug commands, not your program's normal data.

items.prompt: What does setting a breakpoint do?
choices: Permanently deletes that line of code
Tells the chip to freeze when execution reaches that line, so you can look around
Speeds the program up past that point
Sends the variable to the screen automatically
items.explanation: A breakpoint is a "stop here" marker. When the program hits it, the chip pauses and you can inspect everything. It does not change or delete code.

items.prompt: What does 'stepping' mean in a debugger?
choices: Running the program at half speed continuously
Executing one line (or one instruction) and then stopping again
Skipping over broken code
Restarting the program from the top
items.explanation: Stepping runs a single line then pauses, letting you walk through the code and watch each step's effect. It is not the same as just slowing the program down.

items.prompt: You suspect a counter never reaches 10. What is the most direct debugger move?
choices: Add a slow print after every line
Set a breakpoint in the loop and watch the counter variable as you step
Reflash the whole chip
Measure the supply voltage
items.explanation: Breakpoint plus watching the variable lets you see its actual value directly — the fastest way to confirm or disprove your guess. Printing is slower and the other options don't reveal the value.

items.prompt: Why is a debug probe especially valuable on an MCU with no screen?
choices: It adds a screen to the board
It lets you read variables and pause execution without any on-board display or print path
It makes the program run faster
It replaces the need to write correct code
items.explanation: With no screen, the probe is your window into the running program — reading variables and pausing without needing the chip to print anything. It doesn't speed up code or excuse bugs.

items.prompt: You pause the chip at a breakpoint while a motor is spinning and sensor data is arriving. What is the catch?
choices: The motor and incoming data also pause and wait for you
The program freezes, but the outside world keeps going — so paused debugging can mislead you about timing-sensitive behavior
Pausing damages the chip
Variables become unreadable while paused
items.explanation: Pausing stops the program's clock, not the real world. The motor keeps spinning and data keeps coming, so a breakpoint can distort or hide timing bugs even though it is great for logic bugs.

items.prompt: Which kind of bug is the pause-and-step debugger best suited for?
choices: A timing race that only happens at full speed
A logic error, like wrong math or a loop that never runs
A signal that has electrical ringing
A bus sending the wrong bytes
items.explanation: Logic errors are ideal: pause and inspect to see exactly what the code computed. Timing races, electrical shape, and bus bytes are better seen with tools that don't stop the program.

items.prompt: A nice property of debugging over SWD is that, to inspect a variable, you generally do NOT need to:
choices: Connect a debug probe
Add extra print code to your program just to see that value
Set a breakpoint
Have power to the board
items.explanation: The probe reads memory directly, so you can watch a variable without sprinkling print statements into your source. You still need the probe, power, and (usually) a breakpoint to pause.

lessons.title: Logic Analyzer vs Oscilloscope
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Two of your most useful bench tools look at the *wires* instead of your code. They seem similar, but they answer opposite questions. Knowing which to grab saves hours.

**Build.** A **logic analyzer** watches several wires at once and records each as just a **1 or a 0** — above or below a threshold voltage. Because it stores only one bit per wire per moment, it can watch many wires (often 8 or more) cheaply. Its superpower is **protocol decode**: it can take the raw 1s and 0s of a bus like **SPI**, **I2C**, or **UART** (common ways chips talk to each other) and label them as actual bytes, addresses, and acknowledgements. So it answers: *"what bytes went across, and in what order?"*

An **oscilloscope** ("scope") watches a few wires (usually 2–4) but records the **real, continuous voltage shape** over time. It answers: *"what does the electrical signal actually look like?"* — revealing a slow rounded edge, ringing, or a tiny **glitch** (an unwanted short pulse) that a logic analyzer would quietly round off to a clean 1 or 0.

So: **logic analyzer = "what bytes," oscilloscope = "what shape."** They are partners. A flaky I2C read? Start with the logic analyzer to find *which* byte fails or whether the device acknowledges. If the bytes look impossible — the device should have replied but didn't — put the scope on that wire to see if the voltage even reaches a clean high.

**Recap.** Reach for the logic analyzer to read and decode the bytes on a bus; reach for the oscilloscope to inspect the actual voltage shape and catch glitches the digital view hides.

items.prompt: Study the 'same wire, two views' diagram. What does the logic analyzer view leave out that the oscilloscope view shows?
choices: The order of the pulses
The actual voltage shape — ringing and a narrow glitch get flattened into clean 1s and 0s
The number of wires
The names of the signals
items.explanation: The logic analyzer reports only above/below threshold, so the ringing and glitch the scope reveals are flattened into tidy square edges. That is exactly why a scope is needed for signal-quality problems.

items.prompt: Study the SPI decode diagram. Beyond showing the raw wires, what extra thing is the logic analyzer doing?
choices: Measuring the exact voltage of each high level
Decoding the 1s and 0s into labeled bytes like 0x9F and 0xEF
Pausing the CPU at each byte
Charting current draw over time
items.explanation: Protocol decode turns the raw edges into meaningful bytes you can read directly. It does not measure precise voltage (that's the scope) or pause the CPU (that's the debugger).

items.prompt: Your SPI sensor 'occasionally returns garbage.' The logic analyzer decode looks byte-perfect on every capture, yet the fault keeps happening. Which tool should you reach for next, and why?
choices: Stay on the logic analyzer and capture more often — it will eventually show the bad byte
Put an oscilloscope on the clock and data lines, because a marginal edge or ringing can be misread even when the analyzer shows clean 1s and 0s
Use the debugger to single-step the SPI driver until it fails
Replace the sensor immediately
items.explanation: This is the classic 'which tool' call. The analyzer only reports above/below threshold, so a perfectly decoded capture can still ride on a marginal or ringing analog edge. The scope shows the signal-shape problem the digital view hides; capturing more on the analyzer won't reveal it.

items.prompt: What single capability makes a logic analyzer the default choice for debugging SPI/I2C/UART?
choices: It measures voltage more precisely than a scope
Protocol decode: it turns the raw 1s and 0s into labeled bytes, addresses, and acknowledgements
It can pause the CPU
It draws less power from the board
items.explanation: Decoding the bus into readable bytes lets you see the actual transaction instead of guessing from edges. Voltage precision is the scope's strength, not the analyzer's.

items.prompt: When is the oscilloscope the right tool instead of the logic analyzer?
choices: When you want to read which bytes crossed the bus
When the question is about signal shape: slow edges, ringing, or a narrow glitch
When you want to decode an I2C address
When you need to watch eight wires at once
items.explanation: Signal-shape questions need the scope's continuous voltage view. Reading bytes, decoding addresses, and watching many wires are logic-analyzer jobs.

items.prompt: What does a logic analyzer give up compared to an oscilloscope?
choices: The ability to decode protocols
Voltage detail — it sees only above or below a threshold, not the real waveform shape
The ability to trigger on an edge
The ability to watch more than one wire
items.explanation: By storing one bit per sample, the analyzer trades away voltage detail. That is precisely why it can't diagnose ringing or marginal edges, while it keeps its decode and many-channel strengths.

items.prompt: Why can a logic analyzer typically watch 8+ wires while a scope often watches only 2–4?
choices: Logic analyzers are always more expensive
The analyzer stores just one bit (high/low) per wire per sample, which is cheap; a scope stores full voltage detail, which costs much more per channel
Scopes are not allowed to have more channels
Wires for a scope must be longer
items.explanation: One bit per wire is cheap to store, so wide channel counts are easy. Full-resolution voltage capture is expensive per channel, which limits scope channel counts.

items.prompt: An I2C device intermittently fails to acknowledge. What is the sensible order of tools?
choices: Scope first to read the bytes, then analyzer for the shape
Logic analyzer first to find which byte/address the missing ACK lands on; then a scope on that wire only if the bytes look impossible
Debugger only — single-step until it fails
Replace the device, then test
items.explanation: Start with the analyzer to localize the failing transaction (bytes/ACK). Only escalate to the scope's signal-shape view if the decoded bytes don't make sense. The scope can't read bytes for you.

items.prompt: What is a 'glitch' that a scope can reveal but a logic analyzer may hide?
choices: A correctly decoded byte
A tiny unwanted pulse or distorted edge in the signal that gets rounded off to a clean 1 or 0 by the analyzer
A software exception in the CPU
A missing pull-up resistor value
items.explanation: A glitch is a brief, unwanted feature of the actual waveform. The scope's continuous view shows it; the analyzer's threshold view can flatten it into a clean level and miss it entirely.

lessons.title: Measuring Time with a GPIO Toggle
lessons.content:
**Hook.** "How long does this code take?" is one of the most important questions in embedded work, and there is a beautifully cheap way to answer it: flip a pin.

**Build.** A **GPIO** pin (General-Purpose Input/Output — a pin you can drive high or low from software) can act as a tiny event marker. The trick: set the pin **high** at the start of the code you care about, and set it **low** at the end. Now connect a scope or logic analyzer to that pin. The **width of the high pulse is exactly how long that code took.**

Why is this so good? Setting a pin high or low is a single, fast operation — it costs only nanoseconds. Compare that to printing a message, which can take *milliseconds* (thousands of times longer). Because the toggle is so cheap, it barely disturbs the thing you are measuring. This matters most for an **ISR** (Interrupt Service Routine — the short function that runs when an interrupt fires): you can wrap the whole ISR in a high/low toggle and read its duration directly.

You can also measure **jitter** — how much the timing wobbles from one run to the next — by capturing many pulses and watching whether they all line up or vary. Steady pulses mean predictable timing; varying ones mean something is occasionally delaying you.

Hypothesis-test in action: "I think my ISR sometimes runs too long." Toggle a pin around it, capture a few hundred events, and *look* — the worst-case pulse width is your answer, measured, not guessed.

**Recap.** Toggle a GPIO pin high at the start and low at the end of a section, then measure the pulse on a scope. It is the cheapest, most honest way to measure how long code (especially an ISR) takes.

items.prompt: Study the GPIO-toggle timing diagram. What does the width of the high pulse on the pin represent?
choices: The supply voltage of the board
How long the ISR took to run (set high at entry, low at exit)
The number of bytes sent on the bus
The clock frequency of the CPU
items.explanation: The pin is set high at ISR entry and low at exit, so the high-pulse width is exactly the ISR's run time. It is not a voltage, byte count, or clock reading.

items.prompt: What is the basic recipe for GPIO-toggle timing?
choices: Print the time before and after the code
Set a pin high at the start and low at the end of the code, then measure the pulse on a scope
Count the lines of code and multiply by the clock period
Pause the chip with a breakpoint and read a stopwatch
items.explanation: High at the start, low at the end, then read the pulse width — that's the whole technique. Printing is too slow, line-counting is inaccurate, and a breakpoint stops the very thing you're timing.

items.prompt: Why does a GPIO toggle barely disturb the code it measures, while a print statement does?
choices: A toggle uses a special debug-only pin
Setting a pin is a single fast operation (nanoseconds), while printing can take milliseconds — thousands of times longer
Printing is done by the debug probe, not the CPU
A toggle runs on a separate processor
items.explanation: The cost gap is the point: a pin write is nanoseconds, a print is milliseconds. Because the toggle is so cheap, it doesn't meaningfully change the timing you're trying to observe.

items.prompt: What is an ISR, and why is GPIO-toggle timing a great fit for it?
choices: A type of memory; toggling lets you erase it
An Interrupt Service Routine — a short function that runs on an interrupt; toggling lets you measure its run time without slowing it down
A debug protocol like SWD; toggling replaces it
A kind of resistor; toggling tests it
items.explanation: An ISR runs in response to an interrupt and must usually be short. Wrapping it in a cheap pin toggle measures its exact duration without the heavy disturbance a print would cause.

items.prompt: You capture many toggle pulses and notice their widths vary from run to run. What does that variation tell you?
choices: The scope is broken
There is jitter — the timing wobbles, meaning something occasionally delays the code
The pin voltage is too low
The code never runs
items.explanation: Varying pulse widths are jitter: the run time is not perfectly consistent, hinting at occasional delays (e.g., other interrupts). Steady widths would indicate predictable timing.

items.prompt: Why not just add a print at the start and end of the ISR to time it?
choices: Printing is illegal inside an ISR
Printing is so slow it would dominate and distort the measurement, and may itself stall the ISR
Prints can't include numbers
The scope can't see prints
items.explanation: A slow print would add far more time than the ISR itself, wrecking the measurement and possibly delaying real work. The cheap toggle avoids this by costing almost nothing.

items.prompt: To check whether an ISR ever runs too long, what should you look at across many captured pulses?
choices: The average pulse width only
The worst-case (longest) pulse width, since the occasional long run is what breaks deadlines
The shortest pulse width
The voltage of the high level
items.explanation: In real-time work the worst case matters most — a rare long run can miss a deadline even if the average looks fine. Capturing many events lets you find that worst-case width.

items.prompt: Applying the hypothesis-test mindset to timing, what is the right sequence?
choices: Assume the ISR is fine because the code is short
Guess the ISR may run long, toggle a pin around it, capture many events, and read the measured worst case
Rewrite the ISR first, then never measure
Add prints until the bug disappears
items.explanation: You form a clear guess, pick the cheap matching tool (the toggle), and measure. Short code can still be slow (e.g., a hidden delay), so you confirm by measuring rather than assuming.

items.prompt: Which tool do you connect to the toggled pin to actually read the pulse width?
choices: A debugger over SWD
An oscilloscope or logic analyzer
A multimeter set to average voltage
Another MCU's UART
items.explanation: A scope or logic analyzer shows the pin's high/low timing directly, so you can read the pulse width. A multimeter only shows an average, and a debugger would stop the timing.

lessons.title: Low-Overhead Logging: printf vs SWO/ITM and RTT
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Printing messages feels like the safe, familiar way to debug. On a microcontroller it is often a trap — and understanding why teaches a deep lesson about *not disturbing what you measure*.

**Build.** The classic `printf` on bare metal does two slow things. First it builds the text. Then it pushes the bytes out a slow serial wire (a **UART**) one at a time, and the CPU usually **waits** for each byte to leave. A single line can cost milliseconds — an eternity for a chip. That delay sits right inside the code you instrumented, so it *changes the timing of the very bug you are chasing*. This is the heart of a famous problem: **a slow print can hide a timing bug** by perturbing the timing enough that the bug stops appearing.

Lower-overhead alternatives let you log without that heavy cost:

- **SWO/ITM trace.** ARM Cortex-M chips have a built-in trace unit (ITM). Your code writes a value to a register and it goes out a single dedicated pin (SWO) to the debug probe. The CPU does one quick write and moves on — no slow wait.
- **RTT (Real-Time Transfer).** Your code drops a few words into a small buffer in the chip's RAM (very fast). The debug probe reads that buffer *out of the side*, over the debug link, while the program keeps running. No special pin and very little disturbance — it is the everyday workhorse for fast logging.

The big idea: low-overhead logging gets information *out* without stopping the chip or stealing its time, so the bug you're hunting still behaves the same. (Conceptually, that's the goal; the exact setup varies by chip and probe.)

**Recap.** Blocking `printf` over a UART is slow enough to distort timing and hide bugs. Prefer low-overhead trace (SWO/ITM) or RTT, which log with so little cost that they barely disturb the running program.

items.prompt: Study the 'why printf can hide a bug' diagram. What is the key difference between the printf path and the trace/RTT path?
choices: The printf path is faster
The printf path makes the CPU wait (milliseconds) while bytes leave a slow wire; the trace/RTT path writes quickly and lets the CPU keep running
The trace path needs no debug probe at all
Both paths cost exactly the same time
items.explanation: The diagram contrasts blocking-and-waiting (printf over UART) with write-and-continue (trace/RTT). The whole point is that the low-overhead path doesn't make the CPU sit and wait.

items.prompt: A race bug only shows up at full speed. You add a printf in the suspect code and the bug disappears. What most likely happened?
choices: The printf fixed the bug
The printf's slow delay changed the timing enough to mask the race — the bug is still there, just hidden; use a low-overhead trace or a pin toggle instead
The compiler removed the race
The serial hardware corrected the data
items.explanation: This is the central 'why printf can hide a timing bug' lesson. Blocking printf adds milliseconds that perturb the timing you're measuring, so the race stops appearing without being fixed. Low-overhead trace or a GPIO toggle observes without disturbing.

items.prompt: What are the two slow steps of a classic blocking printf on bare metal?
choices: Compiling the code and flashing it
Building the text, then waiting while the bytes go out a slow serial wire one at a time
Reading a sensor and converting it
Pausing the CPU and reading memory
items.explanation: First it formats the string, then it blocks pushing bytes out a slow UART. That blocking wait is what costs milliseconds and disturbs timing.

items.prompt: How does SWO/ITM trace logging avoid the printf bottleneck?
choices: It prints faster by raising the baud rate
Code writes a value to the chip's trace unit and it exits on a dedicated pin to the probe — one quick write, no long wait
It disables interrupts while printing
It stores logs on an SD card
items.explanation: ITM lets the CPU do a single quick register write that the trace pin (SWO) carries to the probe, instead of looping over a slow UART transmit. Raising baud still leaves a blocking, disturbing path.

items.prompt: What makes RTT (Real-Time Transfer) so low-overhead?
choices: It compresses the text before sending
Code just drops a few words into a RAM buffer; the debug probe reads that buffer out over the debug link while the program keeps running
It sends logs over Wi-Fi
It only logs once at startup
items.explanation: RTT's trick is writing to a RAM buffer (cheap) and letting the probe drain it out-of-band. The CPU never waits on a slow wire, so timing stays nearly undisturbed.

items.prompt: You need to log a lot of data while the firmware keeps meeting tight deadlines. Best approach?
choices: Crank the UART baud rate way up and keep using blocking printf
Use a low-overhead method like RTT, where the CPU only writes to RAM and the probe reads it out
Add more breakpoints
Disable interrupts during every print
items.explanation: RTT delivers high throughput while barely disturbing timing. A faster UART is still a blocking path that steals time, and breakpoints/disabling interrupts make timing worse.

items.prompt: What general principle explains why low-overhead logging is preferred for timing-sensitive code?
choices: More text is always better data
Get information out with as little cost as possible, so the act of observing doesn't change the behavior you're observing
Only the debug probe should ever run code
Logging should always stop the CPU first
items.explanation: The aim is to observe without disturbing — the same idea behind the GPIO toggle. Heavy logging changes timing; light logging preserves the behavior you're trying to study.

items.prompt: Your code is full of printf calls you can't rewrite right now, but the blocking UART is wrecking timing. Pragmatic fix?
choices: Delete all logging
Redirect the low-level output of printf so the bytes go into a fast buffer (like RTT) instead of the slow UART
Lower the baud rate so each byte is even slower
Only call printf once per minute
items.explanation: You can keep the printf call sites but point their output at a low-overhead path like an RTT buffer. Same source, but the bytes no longer block on a slow UART.

items.prompt: What does it mean that a UART printf is 'blocking'?
choices: It blocks other programs from running on your PC
The CPU stops and waits for the bytes to finish leaving the slow wire before continuing
It blocks interrupts permanently
It prevents the chip from booting
items.explanation: Blocking means the CPU sits idle waiting for each byte to be sent, instead of doing useful work. That wait is the source of the timing disturbance.

lessons.title: Decoding a Crash and Debugging Cleanly
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Sometimes a program doesn't just misbehave — it *crashes*. On an ARM Cortex-M chip the crash has a name, a **HardFault**, and the good news is a crash is not a mystery: the chip leaves behind clues that point at the exact line that broke.

**Build.** A **fault** is the chip's reaction to something illegal — like using a bad pointer (an address that points nowhere valid) or doing math it can't, such as dividing by zero. When this happens, the chip jumps to a special handler. The most common one is the **HardFault**, a catch-all for serious errors. (There are more specific ones too, like a bus fault for a bad memory access.)

Here's the clue trail. When the fault happens, the chip automatically saves a small set of registers onto the stack. One of them is the **PC** (Program Counter — the address of the instruction that was running). That saved PC is essentially the address of the *faulting instruction*. A tool can translate that address back into a **source line**, so "crash" becomes "line 47 in sensor.c." Other status registers tell you *why* it faulted (bad pointer? divide by zero?). Conceptually: read the saved PC for *where*, read the status registers for *why*.

Two habits keep crashes rare and debugging honest:

- **Assertions.** An `assert` is a built-in check: "this must be true; if not, stop now." It catches a bad assumption at the moment it happens, instead of letting it corrupt things and crash mysteriously later.
- **Non-intrusive vs intrusive.** A tool that observes without changing timing (a pin toggle, RTT) is *non-intrusive*. A tool that stops or slows the chip (a breakpoint, a blocking print) is *intrusive* and can create or hide a bug. A timing bug that appears or vanishes only because you're observing it is nicknamed a **Heisenbug** — a reminder to prefer non-intrusive tools when timing matters.

**Recap.** A HardFault isn't random: the saved PC tells you *where* it crashed and the status registers tell you *why*. Use assertions to catch bad assumptions early, and prefer non-intrusive observation so you don't create Heisenbugs.

items.prompt: Study the exception stack-frame diagram. Which saved register points at the instruction that was running when the fault hit?
choices: R0
The stacked PC (Program Counter)
xPSR
items.explanation: The diagram highlights the stacked PC as the faulting instruction's address. Translating that address to a source line is how a crash becomes a specific line of code.

items.prompt: What is a HardFault on a Cortex-M chip?
choices: A physical crack in the silicon
The chip's catch-all reaction to a serious error, like a bad pointer or illegal operation
A slow but normal part of booting
A warning that the battery is low
items.explanation: A HardFault is the software-level catch-all exception for serious errors such as a bad pointer access or an illegal instruction. It is not hardware damage or a battery warning.

items.prompt: After a HardFault, which clue tells you WHERE the crash happened (which instruction)?
choices: The saved PC (Program Counter), which you translate into a source line
The supply voltage
The number of interrupts pending
The size of the heap
items.explanation: The automatically stacked PC is the address of the faulting instruction; mapping it to source pinpoints the line. Voltage, pending interrupts, and heap size don't locate the fault.

items.prompt: The saved PC tells you where the crash was. What tells you WHY it faulted (e.g., bad pointer vs divide-by-zero)?
choices: The PC again
The fault status registers, which record the cause
The stack pointer alone
The clock frequency
items.explanation: Status registers record the reason for the fault (bad memory access, illegal operation, etc.). The PC gives the location; the status registers give the cause.

items.prompt: Applying the hypothesis-test mindset to a HardFault, what is the clean first sequence?
choices: Add prints everywhere and rerun until it stops
Read the saved PC to find the faulting line, then read the status registers to learn the cause
Comment out code at random until the crash goes away
Increase the stack size and hope
items.explanation: The deterministic recipe is PC → where, status registers → why. That turns a crash into a precise bug report, far faster than random prints or commenting code out.

items.prompt: What is a common cause of a fault that involves a 'bad pointer'?
choices: A variable set to the correct address
Using an address that points to no valid memory (e.g., a null or garbage pointer)
A loop that counts up correctly
A function that returns the right value
items.explanation: A bad pointer holds an invalid address (null or garbage), so accessing through it is illegal and faults. Correct addresses, loops, and returns don't cause this.

items.prompt: What does an assertion (assert) do, and why is it useful?
choices: It speeds up the program
It checks that a condition you expect is true and stops immediately if not, catching a bad assumption right when it happens
It prints the whole program state continuously
It deletes invalid data automatically
items.explanation: An assert verifies an expected condition and traps at once if it's violated, so you catch the problem at its source rather than after it has corrupted state and caused a confusing later crash.

items.prompt: A timing bug appears at full speed but vanishes the moment you set a breakpoint to investigate. What is this called, and what's the lesson?
choices: A compiler bug; switch compilers
A Heisenbug; the act of observing (the intrusive breakpoint) changed the timing, so prefer non-intrusive tools when timing matters
A hardware failure; replace the board
A normal feature; ignore it
items.explanation: A Heisenbug changes or disappears because the intrusive observation altered timing. The takeaway is to use non-intrusive tools (pin toggle, RTT) for timing-sensitive bugs, just like avoiding blocking printf.

items.prompt: Which pair correctly labels the tools as non-intrusive vs intrusive?
choices: GPIO toggle = intrusive; breakpoint = non-intrusive
GPIO toggle and RTT = non-intrusive; a breakpoint and a blocking print = intrusive
Everything is non-intrusive
Only the oscilloscope is intrusive
items.explanation: Cheap, observe-without-stopping methods (pin toggle, RTT) are non-intrusive; anything that halts or significantly slows the chip (breakpoint, blocking print) is intrusive and can distort timing.

items.prompt: Why is the saying 'a crash is not a mystery' justified on Cortex-M?
choices: Because crashes never actually happen
Because the chip automatically leaves clues — the saved PC for where, and status registers for why
Because the debugger rewrites the buggy code
Because all crashes are caused by low voltage
items.explanation: On a fault the chip stacks registers (including the PC) and sets status bits, giving you a location and a cause. Those clues turn a scary crash into a readable bug report.

lessons.title: Living in Kilobytes: Measuring Memory & Avoiding the Heap
lessons.content:
**Hook.** On a laptop you almost never worry about memory — you have gigabytes. A *microcontroller* (the tiny computer-on-a-chip inside a thermostat, a toy, or a smart bulb) has only **kilobytes**. A kilobyte is about a thousand bytes; a gigabyte is about a billion. So a microcontroller has roughly a *million times less* room. When space is that tight, you must be frugal and count every byte.

**Build.** A microcontroller has two separate kinds of memory, and they are two separate budgets:

- **Flash** — where your *program* is stored. It keeps its contents even when the power is off, like a tiny built-in hard drive. Your code and any fixed constants live here.
- **RAM** — *working memory* the program scribbles in while it runs. It is wiped when power goes off. Variables, the *stack* (the scratchpad for function calls), and any *heap* live here.

You do not have to guess how much you use. The build tools print a **size report**: a few numbers telling you how many bytes of flash and how many bytes of RAM your program needs. The rule is simple: **measure first, then cut the biggest thing.**

The single most useful habit for a beginner is: **avoid the heap.** The *heap* is memory you ask for while the program is running and give back later ("grow-on-demand" memory). On a big computer that is fine. On a chip with kilobytes it is risky: requests can fail unpredictably after the device has been running a while, and that is hard to recover from in the field. Instead, use **static buffers** — fixed-size storage you decide on ahead of time, before the program runs. You always know exactly how much it costs.

**Recap.** Microcontrollers live in kilobytes, with flash for code and RAM for working data. Measure usage with the size report, cut the biggest hog, and prefer fixed-size static buffers over grow-on-demand heap memory.

items.prompt: Use the diagram. It shows two separate memory budgets on a microcontroller. Which pairing is correct?
choices: Flash stores the program and keeps it when power is off; RAM is working memory wiped at power-off
Flash is wiped at power-off; RAM keeps the program forever
Flash and RAM are the same memory with two names
Flash holds variables while running; RAM holds the program
items.explanation: Flash is non-volatile storage for code and constants — it survives power-off, like a tiny built-in drive. RAM is volatile working memory for variables and the stack, and is wiped when power is removed. They are two distinct budgets, not the same memory.

items.prompt: Roughly how much memory does a typical microcontroller have, compared to a laptop?
choices: About the same as a laptop
Kilobytes — dramatically less than a laptop's gigabytes
More than a laptop, because it is dedicated to one job
Memory does not exist on a microcontroller
items.explanation: A microcontroller typically has kilobytes (thousands of bytes), while a laptop has gigabytes (billions). That is roughly a million times less, which is exactly why you must budget memory carefully on a microcontroller.

items.prompt: Where is your program kept so that it is still there after you unplug the device and plug it back in?
choices: In RAM
In flash
On the stack
It is re-downloaded every time you plug in
items.explanation: Flash is non-volatile: it retains its contents with the power off, so the program stored there is ready the moment you power on. RAM loses its contents at power-off, and the stack is just a region of RAM.

items.prompt: What is the first thing you should do when you suspect your program is too big to fit?
choices: Guess which feature is biggest and delete it
Check the size report to measure how much flash and RAM you actually use
Buy a bigger laptop
Add more comments to the code
items.explanation: Measure before you cut. The build tools print a size report showing your real flash and RAM usage, so you can target the biggest consumer instead of guessing. Guessing wastes effort and can remove the wrong thing.

items.prompt: What is the 'heap'?
choices: A pile of unused chips
Memory you request while the program runs and give back later (grow-on-demand)
The fastest part of flash
Another name for the program's code
items.explanation: The heap is grow-on-demand memory: the program asks for a chunk while running and returns it later. This flexibility is handy on big computers but risky on a tiny chip. It is not flash, and it is not the code itself.

items.prompt: Why is using dynamic (grow-on-demand) memory discouraged on a tiny microcontroller?
choices: It is illegal in the C language
On a chip with so little RAM, requests can fail unpredictably after the device has run for a while, and that is hard to recover from in the field
It makes the program run faster, which wastes battery
It only works when a laptop is connected
items.explanation: With only kilobytes of RAM, repeatedly asking for and returning memory can leave the free space broken into unusable scraps, so a later request fails at an unpredictable moment in a deployed device — with no easy recovery. It is legal, but unwise on tiny systems; fixed buffers avoid the surprise.

items.prompt: What is a 'static buffer', and why is it preferred on a microcontroller?
choices: A buffer that never moves on the screen; it looks nicer
A fixed-size piece of storage decided ahead of time, so you always know exactly how much memory it costs
A buffer made of static electricity
Memory that grows automatically when you need more
items.explanation: A static buffer has a fixed size chosen before the program runs, so its memory cost is known and guaranteed available. That predictability is exactly what you want on a tiny chip, unlike grow-on-demand heap memory that can run out unexpectedly.

items.prompt: Your program is a little too big for flash. The size report shows one feature's code is by far the largest. What is the most sensible move?
choices: Trim or simplify that largest feature and then re-measure
Rename your variables to shorter names
Delete random small functions until it fits
Move the program into RAM instead
items.explanation: Attack the biggest consumer first — that yields the most savings — then re-measure to confirm. Shorter variable names do not change compiled size, deleting at random is unreliable, and the program must live in flash.

items.prompt: Which statement about flash versus RAM is correct?
choices: Both are wiped at power-off
Flash keeps its contents at power-off; RAM is wiped at power-off
RAM keeps its contents at power-off; flash is wiped
Neither can store anything
items.explanation: Flash is non-volatile (keeps data with power off) and holds your program; RAM is volatile (wiped at power-off) and holds working data while running. That difference is why code lives in flash and scratch data lives in RAM.

lessons.title: What Is a Bootloader? The Program That Runs First
lessons.content:
**Hook.** When you switch on a device, *something* has to run before your main program can. On a microcontroller, that something is often a **bootloader** — a small program that runs first, does a little setup, and then hands control to your real program. Think of a hotel front desk: it greets you first, checks a few things, and then sends you to your room.

**Build.** Your real program is called the **firmware** — simply the software inside the device. ("Firm" because it usually lives in flash and changes rarely, unlike apps on a phone.) The bootloader is a separate, smaller program that lives in its own part of flash, near the start.

Why have a bootloader at all? Because it can do one very valuable job: **load a new version of the firmware.** It can receive a fresh copy of the main program, write it into flash, and then start it. That is how a device can be *updated in the field* — out in the real world, in someone's home — without any special equipment. Your Wi-Fi router, your phone, even a smart bulb gets software updates this way.

So the order of events at power-on is a simple chain:

1. **Power on / reset.** The chip wakes up.
2. **Bootloader runs first.** It may check for a new firmware to install, or just decide everything is fine.
3. **It hands control to the application** — your real firmware — which then runs normally.

The bootloader itself usually stays put and is not changed during an update; only the application part gets replaced. That separation is deliberate: the part that does the updating should not be able to break itself.

**Recap.** A bootloader is a small program that runs first when the chip powers on. Its key power is the ability to receive and install a new version of the firmware (the device's software), letting devices update themselves in the field, then it hands off to the application.

items.prompt: Use the boot-flow diagram. What is the correct order of events after power-on?
choices: Application runs first, then calls the bootloader
Power on → bootloader runs first → it hands control to the application
Power on → application → RAM → flash
The bootloader and application run at the same time, competing
items.explanation: At reset the bootloader runs first (like a front desk greeting you), does a little setup or update check, then hands control to the application. The application does not run before the bootloader, and they do not run simultaneously.

items.prompt: In one sentence, what is a bootloader?
choices: A program that runs first when the chip powers on and can hand control to the main program
The physical boot-shaped connector on the board
A tool that runs only on your laptop to compile code
The largest program on the device
items.explanation: A bootloader is a small program that runs first at power-on, does setup (and optionally installs a new firmware), then hands control to the application. It is not a connector, not a laptop-only tool, and it is usually smaller than the application.

items.prompt: What does the word 'firmware' mean?
choices: The metal case of the device
The software inside the device (its program)
A firm handshake protocol
The hardest part of the silicon chip
items.explanation: Firmware is the software running inside an embedded device — its program. It is called 'firm' because it lives in flash and changes rarely compared to phone apps. It is not the case or the silicon itself.

items.prompt: What valuable job lets a bootloader update a device out in the field?
choices: It can speed up the CPU clock
It can receive a new firmware, write it into flash, and then start it
It can add more RAM to the chip
It can charge the battery faster
items.explanation: The bootloader's update superpower is receiving a fresh copy of the program, writing it into flash, and launching it — no special hardware or trip to a repair shop needed. It cannot add RAM, change battery hardware, or magically overclock the chip.

items.prompt: Which everyday example is firmware updating in the field, the way a bootloader enables?
choices: Your Wi-Fi router or smart bulb receiving a software update
Plugging in a new keyboard
Charging your phone overnight
Turning the volume up on a speaker
items.explanation: A router, phone, or smart bulb installing new software while sitting in your home is exactly field firmware update, made possible by a bootloader. Charging, plugging in peripherals, and adjusting volume do not change the device's software.

items.prompt: During a firmware update, which part of flash usually stays unchanged?
choices: The application
The bootloader
Both are erased every time
Neither part exists after an update
items.explanation: The bootloader normally stays put; only the application is replaced. Keeping the updater itself unchanged is deliberate — the part responsible for installing updates should not be able to overwrite and break itself.

items.prompt: Why is it useful that the bootloader and the application are separate programs in separate parts of flash?
choices: It makes the device heavier
So the part that installs updates is not replaced by the update and cannot break itself
Because two programs always run faster than one
It is required to charge the battery
items.explanation: Separation protects the updater: by leaving the bootloader untouched while only the application changes, you keep a reliable program that can always perform (or retry) updates. It has nothing to do with weight, speed, or charging.

items.prompt: The hotel front-desk analogy maps onto the boot flow how?
choices: The front desk is the application; your room is the bootloader
The front desk greets you first and then sends you to your room — like the bootloader running first and then handing off to the application
There is no order; you go straight to your room
The front desk and the room are the same place
items.explanation: The front desk (bootloader) handles you first, checks things, then directs you to your room (the application). That mirrors the bootloader running first at power-on and then handing control to the real program.

items.prompt: Compared to the application, the bootloader is usually:
choices: Much larger and more complex
Smaller, and stored near the start of flash
Stored only in RAM
Identical in size and purpose
items.explanation: The bootloader is typically a small program placed near the start of flash so it runs first. The application is the larger, full-featured program. The bootloader lives in flash (so it survives power-off), not only in RAM.

lessons.title: How Flash Works & How the Bootloader Jumps to the App
lessons.content:
**Hook.** To update firmware safely you first need to know one quirk of flash memory: you cannot just overwrite it like a notepad. And once a new program is in place, the bootloader has to *jump* to it correctly — do this wrong and the device won't start.

**Build.** *Flash is erase-before-write.* Writing to flash can only change bits in one direction. To truly rewrite a spot, you must first **erase** it back to blank. And erase does not work byte-by-byte — it works on a whole **sector** (a block, often several kilobytes) at a time. So to change a few bytes, you erase the entire block they live in, then write. There is also a wear limit: each sector can only be erased a finite number of times (often tens of thousands), so you avoid hammering one spot over and over — spreading writes out is called **wear leveling**.

Now the **flash layout**. Flash is divided into regions: the **bootloader** sits at the start, and the **application** sits in its own region at a higher address. The diagram shows this split (plus a spare slot we'll use later).

*The jump.* The application is built to live at its own address and starts with two key values at its very beginning: where its **stack** should start, and the address of its **first instruction** (its entry point). To launch the app, the bootloader, in order: (1) points the chip's *vector table* setting at the app's region, so interrupts go to the app's handlers; (2) sets the **stack pointer (SP)** to the app's stack start; (3) sets the **program counter (PC)** — the "what to run next" pointer — to the app's entry point. Setting the PC is the actual jump. Skipping the vector-table step is a classic bug: the app runs until the first interrupt, then crashes.

**Recap.** Flash must be erased (a whole sector at a time) before rewriting, and it wears out, so spread writes. The bootloader and app live in separate flash regions, and the bootloader launches the app by pointing the vector table, setting the stack pointer, and finally setting the program counter to the app's entry.

items.prompt: Use the flash-layout diagram. Which describes it correctly?
choices: The bootloader sits at the start; the application has its own region at a higher address, with a spare staging slot
There is only one region, shared by everything
The application sits before the bootloader
Flash holds only working variables, no code
items.explanation: The layout shows the immutable bootloader at the low address, the active application in its own region above it, and a spare slot for staging a new image. Flash is divided into regions and holds code, not just variables; the bootloader comes first.

items.prompt: What does 'erase-before-write' mean for flash?
choices: You can freely overwrite any byte at any time
To truly rewrite a location you must first erase it back to blank, and erase works on a whole block
Flash must be erased every single time you read it
Erasing and writing are the same operation
items.explanation: Flash writes can only change bits one way, so to genuinely change data you must erase first — and erase happens on a whole sector/block, not per byte. Reading never requires an erase, and erase and write are distinct steps.

items.prompt: You need to change just a few bytes that sit inside one flash sector. What must you do?
choices: Overwrite those few bytes directly
Erase only those few bytes
Erase the whole sector that contains them, then write the data back
Move the bytes into RAM permanently
items.explanation: Erase granularity is a whole sector, and you cannot rewrite bytes in place, so you erase the entire containing sector (saving any other data it held) and then program. You cannot erase just a few bytes, and the data must end up back in flash.

items.prompt: Why do designers avoid erasing the same flash sector over and over, and what is the fix called?
choices: It uses too much electricity; the fix is 'sleep mode'
Each sector can only be erased a limited number of times before wearing out; spreading writes around is called 'wear leveling'
It is too slow; the fix is 'overclocking'
Flash never wears out, so there is no concern
items.explanation: Flash sectors have a finite erase-cycle limit (often tens of thousands), so hammering one sector wears it out. Spreading writes across many locations — wear leveling — extends the flash's life. It is about endurance, not speed or power.

items.prompt: Where do the bootloader and the application live in flash?
choices: In the same bytes, taking turns
The bootloader near the start; the application in its own separate region at a higher address
Both only in RAM
The application near the start; the bootloader is not stored anywhere
items.explanation: Flash is split into regions: the bootloader sits near the start so it runs first, and the application occupies its own region higher up. They do not share the same bytes, and both are stored in flash, not RAM.

items.prompt: What two key values sit at the very beginning of the application, which the bootloader needs to launch it?
choices: The app's name and its version number
Where the stack should start, and the address of the app's first instruction (entry point)
The Wi-Fi password and the device serial number
The amount of free RAM and free flash
items.explanation: The application begins with its initial stack-pointer value and the address of its entry point (first instruction). The bootloader reads these to set the stack pointer and to jump. Names, versions, passwords, and free-space figures are not what the launch sequence needs.

items.prompt: What is the 'program counter' (PC), and how is it used in the jump to the app?
choices: A counter of how many programs are installed; it is reset to zero
The pointer to 'what instruction to run next'; setting it to the app's entry point is the actual jump
A timer that counts seconds since boot
The number of bytes the program uses
items.explanation: The program counter holds the address of the next instruction to execute. When the bootloader sets the PC to the application's entry point, the CPU starts running the app — that is the jump. It is not a count of programs, a clock, or a size.

items.prompt: A bootloader jumps to the app but forgets to point the vector table at the app's region. The app runs fine until its first interrupt, then crashes. Why?
choices: The stack pointer was never set, which only matters during interrupts
Interrupts are still routed to the bootloader's handler list instead of the app's, so the wrong code runs
Forgetting the vector table erases the application
Interrupts cannot happen unless the vector table is wrong
items.explanation: The vector table tells the chip where to find each interrupt's handler. If it still points at the bootloader's table, the first interrupt jumps into bootloader/garbage code relative to the running app, and it crashes. The crash appears only when an interrupt finally fires.

items.prompt: Put the bootloader's launch steps in the right order.
choices: Set the program counter first, then the stack pointer, then point the vector table
Point the vector table to the app, set the stack pointer, then set the program counter to the entry point
Erase the app, then set the stack pointer, then read its name
Only set the program counter; nothing else is needed
items.explanation: The safe order is: point the vector table at the app (so interrupts dispatch correctly), set the stack pointer to the app's stack start, then set the program counter to the entry point — that last step is the jump. Skipping the vector-table or stack-pointer step causes crashes.

lessons.title: Receiving & Writing a New Firmware Image
lessons.content:
**Hook.** So far the bootloader can run first and launch the app. Now for the interesting part: actually *getting* a new program onto the device and writing it into flash. This is the heart of a field update.

**Build.** The new program is packaged as an **image** — just a file containing the bytes of the new firmware (often a `.bin` file). The device receives this image over some **transport**, a link that carries the data:

- **UART** — a simple two-wire serial cable, common for wired updates on a workbench.
- **USB** — the same connector you use for many gadgets; a common style is called *DFU* (Device Firmware Upgrade) mode.
- **OTA** ("over the air") — *wireless*, such as Wi-Fi or Bluetooth. This is how your phone or smart bulb updates with no cable at all.

No matter the transport, the smart place to put the incoming image is **not** on top of the program that is currently running. Recall the flash layout had a **spare slot**. The device keeps running the current app in its slot while it writes the brand-new image into the *spare* slot. This is the safe choice: if the download is interrupted, the working app is still completely intact.

Writing the image obeys the flash rules from the previous lesson: **erase the spare slot first** (a sector at a time), then **program** the new bytes in. Because the image usually arrives in pieces, the device writes it chunk by chunk as the data streams in. Often the device also runs the flash-writing code from RAM, because on many chips the CPU cannot fetch instructions from a flash region while that same region is being erased or written.

**Recap.** A firmware image (the new program's bytes) arrives over a transport — UART, USB, or wireless OTA — and the device writes it into a *spare* slot (erase then program, chunk by chunk) while the current app keeps running, so an interrupted download never harms the working firmware.

items.prompt: Use the diagram of receiving an image. Where does the new image get written, and what is the running app doing meanwhile?
choices: Written over the running app, which must stop
Written into the spare slot (Slot B) while the current app in Slot A keeps running
Written into RAM permanently, never flash
Written into the bootloader region
items.explanation: The incoming image goes into the spare slot while the current app keeps running from its own slot. That way an interrupted download leaves the working app untouched. It does not overwrite the running app or the bootloader, and firmware lives in flash, not permanently in RAM.

items.prompt: What is a firmware 'image'?
choices: A photo of the circuit board
A file containing the bytes of the new firmware program
The icon shown on the device's screen
A picture used to test the camera
items.explanation: In firmware updates, an 'image' is the packaged bytes of the new program (often a .bin file) that will be written into flash. It has nothing to do with pictures or icons.

items.prompt: What does 'transport' mean in the context of firmware update?
choices: A truck that ships the device
The link that carries the image data to the device, such as UART, USB, or wireless
The part of flash that holds the image
Moving the device to a repair shop
items.explanation: The transport is the communication link that delivers the new image — a UART cable, a USB connection, or a wireless OTA link. It is not a vehicle and not a flash region.

items.prompt: What does OTA ('over the air') update mean?
choices: Updating using a wireless link such as Wi-Fi or Bluetooth, with no cable
Updating only while the device is airborne
A type of antenna
Updating by physically swapping the flash chip
items.explanation: OTA means the image arrives wirelessly (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, etc.) with no cable — how phones and smart bulbs update at home. It is not about flying or swapping chips, and it is a delivery method, not an antenna.

items.prompt: Why write the incoming image into a spare slot instead of over the currently running app?
choices: The spare slot is faster to write
So the working app stays intact — if the download is interrupted, the device still has a complete, runnable program
Because the running app's slot cannot be erased
It saves battery
items.explanation: Staging in the spare slot keeps the current app whole and bootable throughout the download. If the transfer fails partway, you have lost nothing — the old app still works. It is about safety, not speed or battery.

items.prompt: Writing the image into the spare slot follows which flash rule?
choices: Write directly; flash needs no preparation
Erase the slot first (a sector at a time), then program the new bytes
Program first, then erase
Only RAM rules apply, never flash rules
items.explanation: Flash is erase-before-write, so the spare slot is erased (sector by sector) and then the new bytes are programmed in. You cannot write without erasing first, and programming before erasing makes no sense.

items.prompt: The image often arrives in pieces. How does the device handle that?
choices: It refuses anything that is not delivered all at once
It writes the image chunk by chunk into the spare slot as the data streams in
It stores every chunk in the bootloader region
It can only accept images smaller than one byte
items.explanation: Because data streams in over the transport, the device programs it into the spare slot piece by piece as chunks arrive. It does not require the whole file at once, and chunks go into the staging slot, not the bootloader.

items.prompt: Why do devices often run the flash-writing code from RAM during an update?
choices: RAM code looks nicer
On many chips the CPU cannot fetch instructions from a flash region while that same region is being erased or written
RAM is the only place code can ever run
It permanently moves the program out of flash
items.explanation: Many chips cannot execute code out of a flash region while that region is mid-erase or mid-write. Running the writer from RAM avoids that conflict so the update can proceed. Code normally runs from flash; this is a special case during writing.

items.prompt: Which is a wired transport, and which is wireless, for delivering an image?
choices: UART and USB are wireless; OTA is wired
UART and USB are wired; OTA is wireless
All three are wireless
All three are wired
items.explanation: UART (a serial cable) and USB are wired transports; OTA is the wireless one (Wi-Fi/Bluetooth). Matching the transport to the situation — cable on a bench, OTA in the field — is part of designing an update path.

lessons.title: Safe Updates: Validate, A/B Swap & Rollback (Don't Brick It)
lessons.content:
**Hook.** The scary part of any update is failure. If the power is cut halfway through, or the new program is broken, the device could end up dead — unable to start at all. That dead state has a name: **bricked** (as useful as a brick). A well-designed update makes bricking nearly impossible. Here's how.

**Build.** Three ideas work together.

*1. Validate before trusting.* Once the new image is in the spare slot, the device checks it before ever running it. A **checksum** (a CRC) is a quick math fingerprint over the bytes: if even one byte got corrupted in transit, the fingerprint won't match, and the image is rejected. A stronger check is a **signature** — a cryptographic stamp proving the image really came from the legitimate maker, not an attacker. Rejecting on any mismatch means you never launch a bad image.

*2. A/B (two-slot) update with rollback.* Keep **two slots**: the running app stays in slot A while the new image goes into slot B (as in the last lesson). After validation, the bootloader switches over to slot B on the next restart. Crucially, the new image starts as "on trial": it must run a quick self-test and **confirm** it works. If it doesn't confirm — say it crashes, or a *watchdog* timer (a safety timer that resets the chip if the program stops responding) fires — the bootloader **rolls back**, returning to the known-good slot A. The diagram shows this whole flow.

Why does this beat bricking on power loss? Because the old app is *never erased while the new one is unproven.* Lose power mid-download, or get a bad image, and slot A is still there to boot. The switch only sticks once the new image proves itself.

*3. Secure boot, in one line:* the bootloader refuses to run any image whose signature isn't valid — so only trusted firmware ever runs.

You rarely build all this by hand. Ready-made systems exist — for example **MCUboot** (a popular open-source bootloader) and chip vendors' built-in update tools — that already implement validate, A/B swap, and rollback.

**Recap.** Avoid bricking by validating the image (checksum for corruption, signature for authenticity), using two slots so the working app is never erased until the new one is proven, and rolling back to the old slot if the new image fails to confirm. Secure boot means only validly signed images run; mature tools like MCUboot provide this off the shelf.

items.prompt: Use the update-and-rollback flow diagram. What happens after the new image is received into Slot B?
choices: It runs immediately with no checks
It is validated; if invalid it is rejected and the device keeps running A; if valid it is booted on trial and rolled back if it fails to confirm
Both slots are erased
The bootloader is replaced
items.explanation: The flow validates the image first; an invalid image is rejected (keep running A, no brick), and a valid one is booted on trial — confirmed if healthy, or rolled back to A by a watchdog/boot counter if it fails. It never runs unchecked, and the bootloader stays put.

items.prompt: What does it mean to 'brick' a device?
choices: To make it run faster
To leave it dead and unable to start at all, as useful as a brick
To add a brick-red case
To double its memory
items.explanation: Bricking means a failed update (or other mishap) leaves the device unable to boot — useless, like a brick. A safe update process is designed specifically to prevent this.

items.prompt: What does a checksum (CRC) tell you about a received image?
choices: Whether the bytes arrived uncorrupted (a fingerprint of the data)
Who the legal owner of the device is
How fast the image will run
The image's color scheme
items.explanation: A CRC is a quick math fingerprint over the bytes. If the image got corrupted in transit, the recomputed fingerprint won't match and the image is rejected. It checks for corruption, not ownership, speed, or appearance.

items.prompt: What does a cryptographic signature add beyond a checksum?
choices: It makes the file smaller
It proves the image genuinely came from the legitimate maker, not an attacker
It speeds up the download
Nothing; it is the same as a checksum
items.explanation: A checksum only catches accidental corruption — anyone could recompute it for a tampered image. A signature is a cryptographic stamp proving authenticity (it came from the trusted signer), which a checksum cannot do.

items.prompt: How does keeping two slots (A/B) plus validation prevent bricking when power is lost mid-update?
choices: It makes the download faster so power loss is less likely
The running app in slot A is never erased while the new image is unproven, so if power is lost mid-download slot A still boots
It stores the image twice in the same slot
It charges the battery during the update
items.explanation: The new image is staged and validated in slot B while slot A keeps running and is never erased until the new image proves itself. So a power cut mid-download leaves slot A intact and bootable — the device simply restarts the old firmware. The switch only sticks after validation and confirmation.

items.prompt: A new image is validated and booted, but it starts as 'on trial'. What must it do, and what happens if it doesn't?
choices: It must confirm it works; if it fails to confirm, the bootloader rolls back to the old slot
It must delete the old slot immediately
Nothing; trial mode lasts forever
It must increase its own version number
items.explanation: The new image runs a self-test and must explicitly confirm it is healthy. If it crashes or never confirms (e.g. a watchdog fires), the bootloader rolls back to the known-good previous slot on the next restart — the anti-brick safety net.

items.prompt: What is a watchdog timer's role in a safe update?
choices: It guards the building where the server lives
It is a safety timer that resets the chip if the program stops responding, which can trigger a rollback if the new image hangs
It counts how many images have been installed
It speeds up the CPU during updates
items.explanation: A watchdog resets the chip if the running program stops 'petting' it, meaning the program has hung. If a freshly installed image hangs before confirming, the watchdog reset lets the bootloader roll back to the known-good slot. It is a safety mechanism, not a counter or speed booster.

items.prompt: An image passes its checksum but its signature does not verify. What is the correct action?
choices: Run it anyway, since the checksum proves it is fine
Reject it and keep running the current app; a valid checksum does not prove the image is from a trusted source
Run it but turn off the watchdog
Ask the user to retype the signature
