items.explanation: A good checksum only means the bytes are uncorrupted, not that they are authentic. A failed signature means the image is unauthenticated (possibly tampered with), so secure boot must reject it and keep the trusted app running.

items.prompt: What is 'secure boot', in one line?
choices: Booting only after the user logs in
The bootloader refuses to run any image whose signature is not valid, so only trusted firmware runs
Booting as fast as possible
Booting only when plugged into a charger
items.explanation: Secure boot means the bootloader checks each image's signature and runs it only if it is validly signed by the trusted maker — so only trusted firmware ever executes. It is about authenticity, not login, speed, or charging.

items.prompt: Why might you use a ready-made system like MCUboot instead of writing all the update logic yourself?
choices: It is required by law
It already implements validation, A/B swap, and rollback correctly, so you avoid reinventing tricky, safety-critical logic
It makes the device physically larger
It is the only way to turn on an LED
items.explanation: MCUboot is a popular open-source bootloader that already provides signed-image validation, A/B swap, and rollback — logic that is subtle and easy to get dangerously wrong. Using it (or a vendor's built-in tool) saves effort and reduces bricking risk. It is a choice, not a legal requirement.

subtopics.title: DSP on Microcontrollers - Bridge to Radar

lessons.title: What DSP Is and Why a Small Chip Can Do It
lessons.content:
## Hook

When you put on noise-cancelling headphones, a tiny chip listens to the noise around you and quietly cancels it out. When a music app shows bars dancing to the beat, a chip is measuring the sound. When a phone waits for "OK Google," it is constantly checking a stream of sound for one pattern. All of these are **DSP** at work.

## Build

**DSP** stands for **Digital Signal Processing**. A **signal** is just something that changes over time, like sound, light, or a voltage. "Digital" means we have already turned that signal into a stream of numbers called **samples** (snapshots of the signal taken many times per second). DSP is simply **using math on that stream of numbers to clean up or analyze the signal**: removing noise, or finding which tones (frequencies) are inside it.

The simplest, most useful piece of math is a **filter**. Imagine each new sample is a little bumpy because of random noise. If you replace each sample with the **average** of itself and its neighbors, the sharp random bumps get smoothed away while the real shape stays. That averaging is a filter, and it is the heart of DSP.

For a long time you needed a special **DSP chip** to do this fast enough. That changed when ordinary microcontrollers (small computer chips, **MCUs**) got two helpers. First, a built-in **FPU** (Floating-Point Unit), hardware that does decimal math quickly. Second, special **DSP instructions** that crunch the multiply-and-add step that filters live on. On top of that, ARM gives away **CMSIS-DSP**, a free library (a ready-made toolbox) of functions like filters and the FFT, so you call `arm_fir_f32(...)` instead of writing the math yourself.

## Recap

DSP is doing math on a stream of digital samples to clean up or analyze a signal, and a filter (like smoothing by averaging) is the first tool. Modern MCUs can do this in real time thanks to a built-in FPU, DSP instructions, and the ready-made CMSIS-DSP library.

items.prompt: What does DSP (Digital Signal Processing) actually mean?
choices: Using math on a stream of digital samples to clean up or analyze a signal
A type of memory chip used to store programs
A wireless protocol for connecting sensors
A way to draw graphics on a screen
items.explanation: DSP is processing (doing math on) a signal that has been turned into a stream of numbers called samples. The goal is usually to clean it up (remove noise) or analyze it (find what is in it). The other options are unrelated hardware or communication ideas.

items.prompt: In DSP, what is a 'sample'?
choices: A free trial version of the software
A single numerical snapshot of the signal taken at one instant in time
The loudest part of a sound
A small piece of program code
items.explanation: A sample is one measurement of the signal at a moment in time. Taking many samples per second gives the stream of numbers that DSP works on. It has nothing to do with trial software or code.

items.prompt: A simple smoothing filter often works by replacing each sample with what?
choices: The largest of all the samples seen so far
Zero, to erase the noise
The average of itself and its neighboring samples
A random number
items.explanation: Averaging each sample with its neighbors smooths out the fast random bumps (noise) while keeping the slow real shape of the signal. Taking the largest value or zeroing samples would distort or destroy the signal, not clean it.

items.prompt: Why is smoothing (averaging) called 'filtering'?
choices: Because it removes the unwanted fast wiggles (noise) and lets the wanted slow signal pass through
Because it deletes every other sample to save space
Because it changes the signal into text
Because it only works on water signals
items.explanation: A filter lets some things through and blocks others. A smoothing filter blocks the fast random noise and lets the slower real signal pass, just like a coffee filter blocks grounds and lets liquid through.

items.prompt: What does the FPU (Floating-Point Unit) inside a modern microcontroller do?
choices: It stores the program permanently
It connects the chip to the internet
It is hardware that does decimal (floating-point) math quickly
It controls the power supply voltage
items.explanation: The FPU is dedicated hardware for fast decimal math. Because DSP does huge numbers of multiplications and additions on decimal values, having an FPU makes real-time DSP practical. It is not memory, networking, or power hardware.

items.prompt: What is CMSIS-DSP?
choices: A brand of microcontroller
A free, ready-made library of DSP functions (filters, FFT, math) you can call from your program
A debugging cable
A type of sensor
items.explanation: CMSIS-DSP is a free software toolbox from ARM containing tested, fast DSP building blocks like filters and the FFT. You call its functions instead of writing the tricky math yourself. It is software, not a chip, cable, or sensor.

items.prompt: Which everyday device is a good example of DSP cleaning up a signal?
choices: A mechanical wind-up clock
Noise-cancelling headphones
A paper notebook
A simple light switch
items.explanation: Noise-cancelling headphones use DSP to measure surrounding noise and produce a sound that cancels it, leaving cleaner audio. A wind-up clock, notebook, and light switch involve no signal processing.

items.prompt: Before MCUs gained an FPU and DSP instructions, what was usually needed for real signal processing?
choices: Nothing, any chip could always do it instantly
A separate, dedicated DSP chip built for the job
A faster internet connection
A larger battery
items.explanation: Real-time DSP used to require a separate, specialized DSP chip because ordinary microcontrollers were too slow at the heavy math. Modern MCUs with an FPU and DSP instructions can now often do the job themselves.

items.prompt: Why is calling a CMSIS-DSP function like arm_fir_f32 usually better than writing the filter math yourself?
choices: It is already written, tested, and tuned to run fast on the chip, so you avoid mistakes and get good speed
It makes the program run on any operating system without a chip
It removes the need for any samples
It automatically charges the battery
items.explanation: The library functions are pre-written and optimized to use the chip's FPU and DSP instructions, so you get correct, fast results without reinventing tricky math. They still need samples and hardware to run.

lessons.title: The FFT and the Real-Time Sampling Pipeline
lessons.content:
## Hook

Look at the bouncing bars on a music app's equalizer. The tall bars on the left are the deep bass; the ones on the right are the high notes. Something is taking the music and asking: *which frequencies (pitches) are in this sound right now, and how strong is each one?* The tool that answers that is the **FFT**.

## Build

A recorded signal is a wave that wiggles up and down over time. That is the **time domain**: amplitude versus time. But often the useful information is *which tones are inside the wave*. The **FFT** (Fast Fourier Transform) takes a block of samples and reports the strength of each frequency. The result is the **frequency domain**: a row of bars, where a tall bar (a **peak**) means that frequency is strongly present, and a flat region means that frequency is absent. So in one sentence: **an FFT tells you what frequencies a signal is made of, and how much of each.**

A microcontroller can run the FFT in real time, but first it has to collect samples cleanly and steadily. The classic **real-time pipeline** ties the whole topic together:

1. A **timer** fires at a steady rate and tells the **ADC** (Analog-to-Digital Converter, the part that turns a voltage into a number) to grab one sample.
2. **DMA** (Direct Memory Access, a helper that copies data without bothering the CPU) drops each new sample straight into a **buffer** (a block of memory).
3. When the buffer is full, the **CPU** runs a filter or an FFT on it.
4. The result is used: shown on a display, sent out, or used to make a decision.

Why bother with the timer and DMA? If you instead read the ADC in a simple loop, every sample is delayed by whatever else the CPU happens to be doing, so the spacing between samples is uneven and the FFT result is wrong. A hardware **timer** guarantees a perfectly even rate, and **DMA** moves the data while the CPU is busy elsewhere, so no sample is ever missed.

## Recap

The FFT turns a wave in time into a picture of the frequencies inside it (peaks = present tones). To feed it good data, a timer triggers the ADC at a steady rate, DMA collects the samples into a buffer, and then the CPU processes the buffer.

items.prompt: What does an FFT tell you about a signal?
choices: How long the signal will last
Which frequencies (tones) are present in the signal and how strong each one is
What color the signal is
Where the signal is stored in memory
items.explanation: The FFT converts a block of samples into a list of frequency strengths, revealing which tones make up the signal and how much of each. It does not report duration, color, or memory location.

items.prompt: On an FFT result, what does a tall bar (a peak) at some frequency mean?
choices: That frequency is strongly present in the signal
The signal is about to stop
There is an error in the program
That frequency has been removed
items.explanation: A peak means that tone is strongly present. A flat region means that frequency is weak or absent. Peaks are not errors and do not indicate removal.

items.prompt: The 'time domain' view of a signal shows what?
choices: A list of memory addresses
The signal's amplitude (its up-and-down value) as it changes over time
The temperature of the chip
The strength of each frequency
items.explanation: The time domain plots how the signal's value changes over time, the raw wave you record. Showing the strength of each frequency is the frequency domain, which the FFT produces.

items.prompt: Why use a timer plus DMA to gather samples instead of just reading the ADC in a loop?
choices: Because loops are not allowed in C
Because the timer guarantees perfectly even spacing between samples and DMA collects them without missing any while the CPU is busy
Because DMA makes the ADC use less electricity
Because a loop would store the samples in the wrong language
items.explanation: A hardware timer fires at an exact steady rate, so samples are evenly spaced (which the FFT needs to be correct), and DMA moves each sample into memory on its own, so none are lost even if the CPU is doing other work. A read-in-a-loop approach gives uneven spacing and can miss samples.

items.prompt: What is the job of the ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter) in the pipeline?
choices: It turns a real-world voltage into a number (a sample)
It stores the finished results on disk
It draws the spectrum on the screen
It generates the timer signal
items.explanation: The ADC measures an analog voltage and converts it into a digital number, producing the samples DSP works on. It does not store, draw, or generate timing.

items.prompt: What does DMA (Direct Memory Access) do in this pipeline?
choices: It performs the FFT math
It copies each new sample into a memory buffer without using the CPU
It decides which frequencies matter
It supplies power to the ADC
items.explanation: DMA is a helper that moves data (here, samples from the ADC into a buffer) without making the CPU do the copying. That frees the CPU and prevents missed samples. It does not do the FFT math or supply power.

items.prompt: What is the correct order of the classic real-time DSP pipeline?
choices: CPU processes, then timer fires, then ADC samples, then DMA copies
Timer triggers ADC to sample, DMA copies samples into a buffer, then the CPU processes the buffer
DMA samples, ADC processes, timer displays the result
CPU samples in a loop, then the FFT triggers the timer
items.explanation: The flow is: timer triggers the ADC at a steady rate, DMA collects the samples into a buffer, and once the buffer is full the CPU runs the filter or FFT. The other orders mix up the roles of each part.

items.prompt: What is a 'buffer' in this context?
choices: A polishing cloth for the chip
A block of memory that holds a group of samples until they are ready to be processed
A type of frequency
The screen that shows the spectrum
items.explanation: A buffer is a chunk of memory used to hold samples as they arrive, so the CPU can process them as a group (a block) rather than one at a time. It is not a physical cloth, a frequency, or a display.

items.prompt: If samples are collected at uneven time spacing, what happens to the FFT result?
choices: It becomes faster but still correct
It can be wrong or misleading, because the FFT assumes evenly spaced samples
Nothing changes
The signal turns into text
items.explanation: The FFT assumes samples are taken at a steady, even rate. Uneven spacing distorts the frequency picture, which is exactly why a hardware timer is used to keep the spacing exact.

lessons.title: Number Formats, the Compute Budget, and When the MCU Is Not Enough
lessons.content:
## Hook

A chip doing real-time DSP is in a quiet race against the clock. Samples keep arriving whether or not the math from the last batch has finished. If the chip falls behind, samples pile up and get lost. Understanding that race, and the choices that affect it, is the difference between a system that works and one that drops data.

## Build

**How numbers are stored.** There are two ways to keep the sample values. **Floating-point** (often written `float` or `f32`) stores decimals directly and is easy to use, handling tiny and huge values without fuss; on a chip with an FPU it is fast and the natural default. **Fixed-point** (formats called **Q15** or **Q31**, just integers treated as fractions) was the old way before FPUs existed. Fixed-point can be faster and use less power on a chip without an FPU, but you have to manage the scaling carefully or numbers overflow. The simple rule: if the chip has an FPU, use float; only reach for fixed-point when you have no FPU or must save every bit of power.

**The compute budget.** Samples arrive at a steady rate, $f_s$ samples per second. The CPU works on them in blocks of $N$ samples. A new block fills up every $T = N / f_s$ seconds. That is your deadline: all the math on one block (the filter, the FFT, the magnitude) must finish before the next block is ready. If it does not finish in time, you fall behind and lose data. So the budget is a simple question: *does the math for one block fit in the time it takes the next block to arrive?*

This creates a trade-off. A **bigger block** $N$ gives a finer, more detailed frequency picture, but it takes more math and adds delay. A **higher sample rate** $f_s$ captures faster signals, but shrinks the deadline. You cannot push all of them at once on one chip.

**When the MCU is not enough.** If you need very high sample rates, very large FFTs, or many channels at once, even a fast MCU runs out of time. Then you move the work to a dedicated **DSP** chip, or an **FPGA** (Field-Programmable Gate Array, hardware you wire up to do many operations in parallel). This same reasoning is exactly what a **radar** receiver lives on: it samples a returning signal on a timer, filters it, runs an FFT, and decides what is out there. That is where this whole topic leads.

## Recap

Use floating-point when the chip has an FPU; use fixed-point only when you must. The core constraint is the compute budget: a block of $N$ samples arrives every $N/f_s$ seconds and the math must finish before the next block. When the budget runs out, move to a DSP or FPGA, which is the same path a radar receiver follows.

items.prompt: On a microcontroller that has an FPU, which number format is usually the sensible default for DSP?
choices: Fixed-point, because it is always faster
Floating-point (float / f32), because the FPU makes it fast and it avoids tricky scaling
Text strings, because they are easy to read
Whole numbers only, because decimals are not allowed
items.explanation: With an FPU present, floating-point is fast and avoids the manual scaling that fixed-point requires, so it is the natural default. Fixed-point only wins when there is no FPU or power is extremely tight.

items.prompt: What is the main downside of fixed-point (Q15/Q31) compared to floating-point?
choices: It cannot store any numbers
You must carefully manage scaling, or values can overflow and become wrong
It only works on the internet
It always uses more power
items.explanation: Fixed-point packs values into integers treated as fractions, so the programmer must track scaling to avoid overflow. Its advantages are speed and low power on chips without an FPU; the cost is this extra bookkeeping.

items.prompt: Samples arrive at rate fs and are processed in blocks of N samples. How long is the deadline to process one block?
choices: N times fs seconds
fs divided by N seconds
N divided by fs seconds
Exactly one second, always
items.explanation: A block of N samples fills in T = N / fs seconds, so that is how long you have to finish processing one block before the next is ready. For example, 1000 samples at 1000 samples/second fill in 1 second.

items.prompt: What happens if the math for one block takes longer than the time it takes the next block to arrive?
choices: The chip speeds itself up automatically
Nothing, extra blocks wait politely forever
The system falls behind and starts losing samples (data)
The samples turn into floating-point automatically
items.explanation: If processing is slower than the arrival rate, blocks pile up faster than they can be handled and data is lost. Meeting the deadline N/fs every time is what keeps the system real-time.

items.prompt: Choosing a bigger block size N has which combined effect?
choices: A finer, more detailed frequency picture, but more math per block and more delay
Less detail and less math
A higher sample rate automatically
No effect at all
items.explanation: A larger N gives finer frequency detail (more, narrower bins) but requires more computation and adds latency because you wait longer for the bigger block to fill and process. It does not change the sample rate.

items.prompt: Raising the sample rate fs lets you capture faster signals, but what is the cost?
choices: It makes the FFT meaningless
Blocks fill faster, so the deadline to finish the math gets shorter
It uses no extra resources
It permanently disables the timer
items.explanation: A higher fs means each block of N samples fills sooner (T = N/fs is smaller), shrinking the deadline. So you trade a tighter time budget for the ability to capture faster signals. The FFT still works.

items.prompt: When the demands (very high sample rates, huge FFTs, many channels) exceed what an MCU can finish in time, what is the usual next step?
choices: Give up on the project
Move the heavy processing to a dedicated DSP chip or an FPGA
Add more buttons to the device
Lower the screen brightness
items.explanation: When even a fast MCU cannot meet the deadline, you offload to hardware built for the job: a dedicated DSP, or an FPGA that does many operations in parallel. Adding buttons or dimming the screen does nothing for the compute budget.

items.prompt: What is an FPGA (Field-Programmable Gate Array), in beginner terms?
choices: A kind of battery
Hardware you can wire up to do many operations in parallel, useful when one CPU cannot keep up
A software library like CMSIS-DSP
A type of display
items.explanation: An FPGA is reconfigurable hardware: you configure its internal logic to perform many operations at the same time, which is helpful when a single CPU cannot meet a tight DSP deadline. It is not a battery, a library, or a display.

items.prompt: Why is DSP on an MCU described as the 'bridge to radar'?
choices: Because radars are built only from microcontrollers
Because a radar receiver does the same thing: sample on a timer, filter, run an FFT, then decide what is present
Because radar uses no math at all
Because microcontrollers can transmit radar waves by themselves
items.explanation: A radar receiver follows the same signal chain you learned here: timed sampling, filtering, an FFT, and a decision. That shared structure is why this topic leads naturally toward radar, even though full radar systems use much more powerful hardware.
